user_workflows: Browse and filter clinics: 1) User navigates to clinics.html (optionally with month, skillFocus, maxPrice, sort in the URL). 2) Page calls getClinicFilterOptions to populate filter controls. 3) Page reads URL parameters and sets the current filters and sort. 4) Page calls searchClinics with the derived filters, sort, page, and page_size. 5) User adjusts filters (month, skill focus, price range) or sort; the page updates the URL parameters accordingly and re-calls searchClinics to refresh the list.

View clinic details from the list: 1) After results load via searchClinics, each clinic card shows a ‘View Details’ action. 2) When the user clicks ‘View Details’, the page updates the URL to include clinicId for the selected clinic (preserving existing filter and sort parameters). 3) The presence of clinicId triggers getClinicDetails. 4) The clinic detail panel displays full clinic information while keeping the list and filters visible. 5) Closing the detail view removes clinicId from the URL and hides the detail panel.

Arrive directly on a clinic detail: 1) User opens a shared link such as clinics.html?clinicId=… optionally with month/skillFocus/maxPrice/sort. 2) Page calls getClinicFilterOptions, then sets filter state from any URL parameters. 3) Page calls searchClinics so the list context is available. 4) Because clinicId is present, page calls getClinicDetails and immediately opens the detail panel. 5) User may adjust filters to explore other options while still keeping or changing the selected clinic.

Compare multiple clinics: 1) User runs a search with searchClinics and sees a list of results. 2) On each clinic card, user can toggle an ‘Add to Compare’ control. 3) The component maintains a compare selection set in page state and synchronizes it to a URL parameter (for example, compareIds) representing selected clinic IDs from the current search results. 4) A comparison component reads the current selection (from in-memory state and/or compareIds) and displays a side-by-side comparison using fields from searchClinics (name, division, dates, total_training_hours, price, location, skill_focus). 5) User can remove clinics from the comparison, which updates the selection and the URL parameter.

Register for a clinic from the detail view: 1) With clinicId selected and details loaded via getClinicDetails, the registration form is displayed within the detail panel. 2) User fills in player fields (e.g., name, date of birth) and guardian fields (e.g., name, phone, email, preferred contact method). 3) On form submit, the page calls submitClinicRegistration with clinicId and the nested player and guardian objects. 4) On a successful response, the page shows the confirmation_message, status, and amount_paid. 5) The page may update the URL to reflect registration status (for example, adding a lightweight flag parameter tied to this session) so the confirmation view can be restored on reload without storing anything locally.

Refine search with pagination: 1) After searchClinics returns results, the pagination control uses the total count and current page to show navigation actions (next, previous, direct page selection as supported by the UI). 2) When the user changes the page, the page parameter in the URL is updated. 3) The page calls searchClinics again with the updated page and page_size while preserving filters and sort, refreshing the clinic list and comparison selections that refer to visible clinics.
interactions: Filter changes: When the user changes a filter (month, skill focus, price range), the filter component updates the corresponding URL parameters (month, skillFocus, maxPrice) and resets the page parameter to the first page. This triggers a new call to searchClinics with updated filters.
Sort changes: When the user selects a different sort option, the sort parameter in the URL is updated, page is reset to the first page, and searchClinics is called with the new sort value.

Pagination clicks: When the user clicks next/previous or a specific page number, the page parameter in the URL is set or updated. searchClinics is then called with the updated page while reusing the current filters and sort.

View details interaction: Clicking ‘View Details’ on a clinic card updates the URL to include clinicId for that clinic while preserving all search-related parameters. This triggers getClinicDetails and opens the detail panel component. Closing the detail panel clears clinicId from the URL.

Comparison selection toggles: On each clinic card, an ‘Add to Compare’ control toggles the clinic’s inclusion in the in-memory comparison selection. The component also encodes the selected clinic IDs into a URL parameter (for example, compareIds). The comparison table component reads the current selection to render a side-by-side comparison. Removing a clinic from comparison updates both the in-memory set and the URL parameter.

Registration form submission: Within the detail panel, submitting the registration form calls submitClinicRegistration with the current clinicId and the form’s player and guardian objects. While the call is in progress, the form may show a pending state, then either display the confirmation_message and status from the response or present validation/submit errors, without hardcoding any success text or amounts.

Breadcrumb navigation: Clicking the breadcrumb link labeled as the home page navigates to index.html without carrying over any clinic-specific URL parameters, relying on the separate page’s logic to initialize its own state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and live interface calls, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence. On initial load, the component tree first invokes getClinicFilterOptions to fetch available months, skill focus options, price ranges, and sort choices. After these options are available, the page reads URL parameters to construct the initial search state. The month parameter (if present) is mapped directly to the selected month filter; otherwise, the filter component selects a default month derived from the options returned by getClinicFilterOptions (for example, by choosing the first or a designated default entry). The skillFocus parameter (if present) is mapped to the selected skill focus value; if absent, a default from skill_focus_options is selected, such as an option that represents no specific focus if provided by the API. The maxPrice parameter is parsed as a numeric value and mapped to the price filter; if it is missing or invalid, the price filter is treated as unbounded or set to a neutral default indicated by price_ranges from getClinicFilterOptions. The sort parameter is mapped to the current sort control; if missing or invalid, the sort control selects a default sort option from sort_options (for example, the first or a specifically indicated default). For pagination, the page parameter (defined for this page) is read and converted to a positive integer; if missing or invalid, it defaults to the first page. These derived values (filters, sort, page) are used to call searchClinics. Whenever the user changes a filter or sort in the UI, the corresponding URL parameters (month, skillFocus, maxPrice, sort) and page are updated so that the URL always encodes the current search. Clinic detail state relies on the clinicId URL parameter: when clinicId is present, getClinicDetails is called and the detail panel opens; when clinicId is omitted or cleared, the detail panel is hidden without affecting the list. The compare selection set is represented both in memory and, optionally, via a dedicated URL parameter such as compareIds, containing one or more clinic IDs separated by a delimiter. On load, if compareIds is present, the comparison component translates it into a selection set but only surfaces clinics that are present in the current searchClinics results (IDs not found in the current result page are ignored for display while still leaving compareIds unchanged). Registration state is not permanently stored in the URL, but after a successful submitClinicRegistration call, the page may optionally set a lightweight query parameter (such as a registration flag) that allows re-rendering the confirmation message after a refresh for that browser context, without persisting any payment or personal details in the URL. In all cases, updating URL parameters is the single source of truth for search, sort, pagination, and selected clinic, enabling shareable and reload-safe URLs.

components.functionality: Displays the navigation trail for the Clinics & Camps page and enables navigation back to the home page.
event_handlers: onClickHome: Navigate to index.html without passing any clinic-specific parameters; the browser location is set directly to index.html.

components.functionality: Manages month, skill focus, and price filters for the clinics search, sourcing options from getClinicFilterOptions and reflecting URL parameter values.
event_handlers: onInit: Call getClinicFilterOptions, then read month, skillFocus, maxPrice URL parameters and set initial selected options accordingly (falling back to defaults derived from the returned options when parameters are missing or invalid). After initialization, trigger an initial searchClinics call with the derived filters and current sort and page.

onMonthChange: When the user selects a month option, update the month URL parameter to the selected month_value, reset the page parameter to the first page, and trigger searchClinics with the updated filters and existing sort.
onSkillFocusChange: When the user selects a skill focus, update the skillFocus URL parameter, reset the page parameter, and trigger searchClinics with updated filters and existing sort.

onPriceRangeChange: When the user selects a price range, update the maxPrice URL parameter (using the appropriate max_price value from the chosen range or clearing it if a no-limit option is selected), reset the page parameter, and trigger searchClinics with updated filters and existing sort.

components.functionality: Provides sorting options for clinic search results, such as ordering by start date, price, or total training hours, using options returned by getClinicFilterOptions.
event_handlers: onInit: After getClinicFilterOptions has loaded, read the sort URL parameter. If present and matches a sort_options value, set it as the selected sort; otherwise, select a default sort option from sort_options. Trigger searchClinics with the selected sort and current filters.

onSortChange: When the user selects a different sort option, update the sort URL parameter, reset the page parameter to the first page, and call searchClinics with the updated sort and the current filter values.

components.functionality: Displays the list of clinics returned by searchClinics, showing summary information and actions for details, comparison, and registration entry points.
event_handlers: onSearchRequest: Called whenever filters, sort, or page change. Reads month, skillFocus, maxPrice, sort, and page from the current URL and calls searchClinics with filters derived from these parameters and a page_size determined by the UI configuration or server constraints.

onClinicCardClick: When the user clicks anywhere on a clinic card that is configured to open details, update the clinicId URL parameter with the clinic_id for that card, preserving existing search parameters. This indirectly triggers getClinicDetails via the detail panel component.

onAddToCompareToggle: When the user toggles compare on a card, update an in-memory selection of clinic_ids and synchronize it to a compareIds URL parameter. This event also notifies the comparison components that the selection has changed.

onRegisterClick: Optional shortcut button on the card that behaves like View Details but also focuses the registration section inside the detail panel by ensuring clinicId is set and then scrolling or focusing the registration form component.

components.functionality: Represents a single clinic entry within the results list, binding to one item from searchClinics.clinics and providing actions for detail view, comparison, and registration shortcut.
data_binding: Clinic (via searchClinics.clinics[*].clinic_id, name, skill_focus, division_name, start_date, end_date, price, total_training_hours, location_field_name, description_summary)
event_handlers: onViewDetailsClick: Update clinicId in the URL to this clinic’s clinic_id and preserve other search-related parameters.
onCompareToggleClick: Call the parent list component’s onAddToCompareToggle with this clinic’s clinic_id, updating the comparison selection and related URL parameter.

onRegisterClick: Call the parent list component’s onRegisterClick with this clinic’s clinic_id, which sets clinicId in the URL and ensures the registration form in the detail panel is brought into view.

components.functionality: Controls paging through searchClinics results using the total count and current page, keeping the page number in sync with the URL.
event_handlers: onInit: Read the page URL parameter to set the initial current page, defaulting to the first page when not present or invalid. Trigger searchClinics with this page along with current filters and sort.

onPageChange: When the user selects a new page (next, previous, or specific number), update the page URL parameter and call searchClinics with the updated page, keeping month, skillFocus, maxPrice, and sort unchanged.
onResultsUpdated: When searchClinics results arrive, adjust any internal pagination state (such as the maximum page count) based on searchClinics.total and the current page_size.

components.functionality: Shows detailed information for the selected clinic, including full description, schedule details, total training hours, and price, and contains the registration form for that clinic.
event_handlers: onInitOrUrlChange: Whenever the URL changes, check for clinicId. If clinicId is present and different from the currently loaded clinic, call getClinicDetails with that clinicId and open the panel. If clinicId is absent, close the panel and clear any detail-specific state (keeping the search and comparison state intact).

onCloseClick: When the user closes the detail panel, remove clinicId from the URL while keeping all search, sort, pagination, and comparison parameters unchanged, so that searchClinics results remain visible as they were.

onDetailsLoaded: After getClinicDetails completes, notify the registration form component so it can bind to the loaded clinic_id and display the correct clinic name and price within the form’s summary.

components.functionality: Manages the internal set of clinics selected for comparison and synchronizes it with a URL parameter to make the comparison state shareable and reloadable.
event_handlers: onInitOrUrlChange: On load and whenever the URL changes, read a compareIds parameter (if defined for this implementation). Map the IDs in compareIds to clinics present in searchClinics.clinics and set the internal selection to the intersection of those IDs and the current results. Any IDs in compareIds that are not in the current page are ignored for display in the comparison table but left unchanged in the URL.

onSelectionUpdated: When the user selects or deselects a clinic for comparison (from the clinic card), update the internal selection set, regenerate the compareIds URL parameter to reflect the new set of clinic_ids, and notify the comparison table component to re-render.
onClearSelection: Provide a control to clear all selected clinics from comparison, which empties the internal selection and removes or clears the compareIds parameter from the URL.

components.functionality: Displays selected clinics side by side using fields available from searchClinics results to help users compare dates, total training hours, skill focus, division, location, and price.
event_handlers: onSelectionChange: When notified by the comparison selector that the selection has changed, rebuild the table rows using the subset of searchClinics.clinics whose clinic_id is in the current selection.

onRemoveClinicFromComparison: When the user removes a clinic directly from the comparison table, call the comparison selector’s onSelectionUpdated with that clinic_id removed so that selection state and URL parameters stay synchronized.

components.functionality: Captures player and guardian information for the currently selected clinic and submits the data via submitClinicRegistration, then renders confirmation messaging and status.
event_handlers: onInit: Wait for getClinicDetails to provide a valid clinic_id before enabling input fields. If clinicId is removed from the URL and the detail panel closes, reset the form to a blank state.
onInputChange: Update local form state for player and guardian objects as the user types or selects values (e.g., preferred contact method).

onSubmit: Validate that all required fields for player and guardian are filled according to the form’s rules. If valid, call submitClinicRegistration with clinicId from getClinicDetails.clinic_id and the current player and guardian objects. While awaiting the response, mark the form as submitting to prevent duplicate submissions.

onSubmitSuccess: When submitClinicRegistration returns with success true, display the confirmation_message, status, and amount_paid from the response. Optionally, update a lightweight URL parameter indicating that a registration has just been completed for this clinic, without exposing personal data or payment details in the URL.
onSubmitError: If submitClinicRegistration fails or returns success false, display error feedback and allow the user to correct input and resubmit without altering clinicId or search parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with full-bleed background. Content appears constrained to a centered max-width container with logo aligned to left columns and navigation aligned to right columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global hero and registration/status strip, the Clinics & Camps page continues the same linear, top-to-bottom banding. Content is organized into clear horizontal sections: a slim breadcrumb row, a prominent control strip (filters + sort + comparison selector), the main results band (clinic list paired with a right-side detail panel), then a comparison band, and finally a bold registration band. Within these bands, key interactive elements are often aligned to the far left or far right with generous negative space in the middle, mirroring the logo-left / nav-right / empty-center pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global hero and registration/status strip, the Clinics & Camps page continues the same linear, top-to-bottom banding. Content is organized into clear horizontal sections: a slim breadcrumb row, a prominent control strip (filters + sort + comparison selector), the main results band (clinic list paired with a right-side detail panel), then a comparison band, and finally a bold registration band. Within these bands, key interactive elements are often aligned to the far left or far right with generous negative space in the middle, mirroring the logo-left / nav-right / empty-center pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: All content sits inside a centered max-width container (e.g., ~1120–1280px) on top of a full-bleed dark background. The primary grid behaves like a 12-column layout: functional content typically spans about 8–10 columns in the center, with left-aligned elements (breadcrumbs, filters, list) occupying roughly 60–70% width and right-aligned elements (sort, comparison selector, detail panel, registration summary) occupying 25–35% width. A consistent 40–64px vertical gap separates major sections, and horizontal gaps between left and right content create a visible central corridor of negative space akin to the mockup’s open middle area.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate-to-low: each band focuses on one key task cluster (navigation, filtering/sorting/comparing, scanning clinics, inspecting details, registering), with large breathing room around controls and cards. Items within bars and cards use comfortable padding based on the 8px scale (typically 16–24px internal padding) and avoid cramming multiple unrelated controls into the same line. High-contrast color blocks (dark backgrounds with bold red and accent yellows) are used to separate functionally distinct areas rather than dense borders or micro-UI.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The page continues a Z-pattern: the eye starts at the breadcrumb/title on the top-left, travels right across to the sort + comparison controls, then diagonally down into the main clinic list. From there, attention moves right to the detail panel (echoing the hero’s right-side nav), then down again into the full-width comparison table, and finally to the strong, registration-focused band at the bottom. Left-aligned text anchors each section, while right-aligned accent elements (status labels, key buttons, comparison toggles) provide strong focal points that match the sports-brand, high-impact aesthetic.

layout.overall_description: Below the dramatic global hero and registration status strip, the Clinics & Camps page opens with a slim, left-anchored breadcrumb line that sits comfortably within a dark, full-bleed background and a centered content container. A tall control strip follows, echoing the mockup’s split hero: filters are grouped toward the left half, a wide swath of empty space occupies the middle, and a two-line stack on the far right holds the sort control on top and the comparison selector beneath it, all right-aligned. This band feels like a cockpit for finding the right clinic. Scrolling down, the screen is dominated by the main results band. On desktop, the left 60–65% of the container is a vertical column of bold, full-width clinic cards, while on the right 30–35% sits a sticky clinic detail panel that lines up visually with the right-aligned controls above, separated from the list by a generous central gutter. The user’s gaze moves left-to-right and then down card by card, occasionally snapping to the detail panel when a card is selected. Beneath the list, centered pagination controls form a small, distinct strip before the layout opens into a full-width, dark comparison band: a high-contrast comparison table stretches across most of the content width, framed by ample top and bottom padding so it reads as a major feature, not a cramped table. Finally, a powerful registration section anchors the bottom of the page: a wide, dark panel with the registration form taking the left two-thirds and a right-aligned summary/status column on the far right. This layout preserves the mockup’s drama—big color fields, left/right emphasis, and generous negative space—while organizing the Clinics & Camps tools into a clear, sequential experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the very top of the Clinics & Camps content area, directly below the global hero/status strip. It occupies the full width of the centered content container but uses only the top-left portion visually: the breadcrumb trail is left-aligned and spans roughly 40–50% of the container, with the remaining width to the right left largely empty, reinforcing the low-density, high-contrast aesthetic. There is 24–32px of vertical padding above and below the breadcrumb text, and about 40–48px of vertical space separates it from the control strip below. The background matches the page’s dark main background, relying on typography and subtle contrast rather than a card or bar treatment, so it feels light and secondary in the overall hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow element at the top of the main content stack, scrolling away normally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clinic filters bar forms the left side of a prominent horizontal control strip, immediately below the breadcrumb. This strip echoes the hero’s layout: filters anchored left, related controls stacked to the far right, and intentional empty space in between. The filters occupy roughly the left 55–60% of the content container, arranged in a single horizontal row on larger screens with dropdowns, chips, or pill buttons spaced at 16–24px intervals. The bar itself has a dark, slightly elevated background, with 16–20px of internal vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding. Above and below, there is about 16–24px of breathing room before other elements. On smaller screens, the filters collapse into a vertical stack but remain left-aligned at the top of this band, with sort and comparison moving below. The central 10–15% of the container between the filters and the right-edge controls is mostly negative space, maintaining the open middle from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky control bar on larger screens: once the user scrolls past the breadcrumb, the bar pins to the top of the viewport (with a small offset below the main site nav) so filters, sort, and comparison are always accessible while scrolling results. On mobile, the sticky behavior may be simplified to avoid crowding, but conceptually it remains the main fixed control band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control lives on the far right side of the same horizontal control strip as the filters, mirroring the nav block on the right in the hero. It is right-aligned within the content container, occupying roughly 15–20% of the width on desktop. The control appears on the top line of a right-side vertical stack: the sort label and dropdown (or set of tabs) sit in a compact horizontal group aligned with the top of the filters on the left. A wide horizontal gap—about 10–15% of the container—separates the sort control from the nearest filter element, leaving a deliberate empty middle. Vertical padding matches the filters (16–20px), so the entire strip reads as one unified band. On smaller screens, the sort control drops below the filters but remains right-aligned within its row to preserve the left-right tension characteristic of the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the sticky control bar: it moves with the filters bar and remains pinned at the top of the viewport while scrolling, always on the far right of that sticky region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clinic results list sits immediately below the control strip and forms the main left column of the page’s central content band. On desktop, it occupies approximately 60–65% of the content container’s width, aligned to the left edge. The list is a vertical stack of clinic cards with consistent spacing between them—typically 24–32px of vertical gap—matching the moderate density of the mockup. To the right of this column is a clear gutter of about 5–8% width, then the clinic detail panel, creating a visual “channel” of negative space between list and details. The top of the first card aligns roughly with or slightly below the bottom of the sticky control strip, so as the user scrolls, cards move behind or under the anchored filters/sort band. The list extends downward as long as there are results, pushing pagination and subsequent sections further down the page, maintaining a simple, linear reading order.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow but effectively becomes the main scrollable content region beneath the sticky control strip and adjacent sticky detail panel. On small screens, it expands to nearly 100% of the container width, with the detail panel folding below or into it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each clinic card is a bold, full-width block within the clinic results list’s left column. Horizontally, a card stretches nearly edge-to-edge within that column (using 16–24px internal padding), leaving only the column’s outer gutters to the container edge and the inner gutter to the detail panel. Vertically, cards are fairly tall to accommodate title, key dates, age groups, brief description, pricing, and a strong call-to-action without feeling cramped—typically 24px padding top and bottom. The card background contrasts clearly with the page background (e.g., a very dark charcoal card on a slightly lighter/darker surrounding, or vice versa) and may incorporate a subtle angled accent or bold side stripe in the league’s brand color to reinforce the sports aesthetic. Content within the card is left-aligned, while actions like “View Details” or “Compare” are placed to the right of the card’s lower area, echoing the overall left-text/right-CTA pattern. Hover or focus states slightly lift the card or brighten its border, and selecting a card visually ties it to the detail panel on the right (e.g., by matching accent color).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Cards are standard, in-flow list items. When a card is selected, it doesn’t move but triggers updates in the sticky detail panel and possibly highlights the card itself; no fixed positioning, but it participates in the dynamic interaction pattern with the panel and comparison tools.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit just below the clinic results list, acting as a slim, centered band before the comparison section. They occupy a narrow horizontal strip that spans the full width of the container but visually concentrate in the center 40–60%: page numbers and next/previous arrows align centrally, with wide empty space on both left and right to maintain the low-density feel. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the last clinic card and the pagination, and another 32–40px gap between pagination and the comparison table below. Controls are sized generously for touch (at least 32–40px hit areas) with 8–16px gaps between page numbers. The background remains consistent with the main page background, keeping pagination visually subordinate to content while still clearly identifiable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow element at the bottom of the results segment. It scrolls with the content and does not stick or float; its role is to mark the transition from the list to the comparison section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clinic detail panel occupies the right side of the main results band, visually echoing the hero’s right-aligned nav block. On desktop, it takes roughly 30–35% of the content container’s width and is right-aligned, with a generous vertical alignment matching the top of the first visible clinic card. A clear inner gutter—about 24–32px—separates it from the list’s right edge, preserving a strong central negative space channel. The panel is a tall, vertically scrollable card-like surface with a dark background and 24px internal padding, featuring the selected clinic’s name at the top, followed by key stats (dates, location, age group, spots remaining), then more detailed copy and a prominent primary action button near the bottom (e.g., “REGISTER” in a high-contrast accent color). The panel’s height typically matches or slightly exceeds the viewport height, with content scrolling inside if needed. When no clinic is selected, the panel may display a simple, centered message or illustration prompting the user to select a clinic from the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger screens, the detail panel is position: sticky within the main content area, pinned to the top of the viewport just below the sticky control strip as the user scrolls through the list. This ensures the selected clinic’s details remain in constant view. On small screens, the panel drops below the list and becomes a full-width, in-flow section that opens either inline after a card or as a full-width expansion, losing the sticky behavior to prioritize vertical reading space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison selector is positioned directly beneath the sort control on the far right side of the control strip, forming a two-line right-side stack that mirrors the hero’s two-line navigation area. Within the rightmost 15–20% of the content container, the comparison selector sits on its own line, right-aligned, with about 8–12px vertical gap below the sort control. It may appear as a toggle, button, or small pill group labeled “Compare Clinics” with an indicator of how many clinics are currently selected, visually treated with a bold accent color border or fill to stand out against the dark control bar. The large empty central region between filters on the left and the comparison selector on the right reinforces the dramatic spacing from the mockup, with only this selector and the sort control occupying the rightmost column of attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Integrated into the sticky control bar: as the bar pins to the top of the viewport, the comparison selector remains visible and accessible in the top-right corner, allowing users to enable or access comparison mode at any time while scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison table sits below the pagination controls as its own full-width section, visually similar to the registration status strip in the hero but taller and more detailed. It spans nearly the entire content container width, centered within the full-bleed dark background, and is wrapped in a strong, high-contrast band—often a slightly different dark tone or with a subtle gradient. The top of the section begins with a left-aligned heading such as “CLINIC COMPARISON” in bold, all-caps typography, sitting about 24–32px from the top edge of the band. The table itself stretches across 80–90% of the container, centered horizontally, leaving consistent left and right margins that create a clear frame. Columns represent clinics (usually 2–4 at a time), and rows list key attributes (dates, levels, price, location, spots, etc.), with strong horizontal rules in a lighter tone to separate rows. There is generous vertical padding—32–48px at the top and bottom of the band—so the table feels like a major, intentional feature, not an afterthought. If no clinics are selected, this band may collapse to a short strip with a center-aligned message, keeping the section visually present but less dominant.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the flow, appearing only when comparison mode is activated or clinics are selected to compare. It scrolls normally, but because of its height and contrast, it occupies a full viewport or more, acting as a destination area users scroll down to from the main list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clinic registration form forms the final major content band on the page, echoing the strong registration strip from the mockup but expanded into a full, functional form. When invoked (e.g., via a primary button in a clinic card or the detail panel), it appears as a wide, dark panel spanning the full width of the content container and visually anchored near the bottom of the page content. Within this band, the layout is split horizontally into a left-heavy form area and a right-side summary/status area: roughly 65–70% of the width on the left is dedicated to the form fields (player info, guardian info, options, payment), arranged in a single-column or two-column grid with 16–24px vertical spacing between field groups; the remaining 30–35% on the right houses a vertically stacked summary card showing the selected clinic details, costs, and a bold status indicator (e.g., “OPEN” or “CLOSED”), all right-aligned to echo the hero’s right emphasis. A generous gutter of 24–32px separates these halves. The entire band has substantial vertical padding (32–48px top and bottom), giving the form room to breathe while still feeling intense and focused due to the high contrast and all-caps labels. Above the form, a clear, left-aligned heading like “REGISTER FOR CLINIC” anchors the section, while key confirmation/submit actions are placed at the bottom right of the form column to align visually with the summary/status on the far right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an in-flow section that either expands inline beneath the detail/comparison areas or loads on its own dedicated page view, depending on implementation. Within the page, it is static (not sticky), but its height typically fills at least a full viewport, encouraging focused, single-task interaction when in view. On mobile, the layout collapses to a single column where the summary/status stack on top or below the form, but the strong top-to-bottom banding and bold heading preserve its prominence.

name: Fields & Reservations
title: Fields & Reservations
description: Page for viewing field availability, filtering bookable one-hour slots, and submitting field reservation requests for team practices and other bookings.

core_features: Load available dates, divisions, fields, and time windows for filtering field reservation slots using getFieldReservationFilterOptions.
Allow users to select a date (including specific weekdays such as Wednesdays) via a date picker bound to the available_dates from getFieldReservationFilterOptions.
Provide filters for division, field, time window, and a lights-only toggle, all mapped to the options returned by getFieldReservationFilterOptions.
Search and display one-hour reservation slots based on the selected filters using searchFieldReservationSlots, showing field names, times, division, and light availability indicators.
Visually distinguish available and unavailable slots in the slots list/calendar based on the is_available property returned by searchFieldReservationSlots.
Allow users to click an available slot to open a reservation form, passing reservationSlotId via the URL (fields.html?reservationSlotId=...).
Fetch detailed information for a specific slot using getFieldReservationSlotDetails when reservationSlotId is present in the URL.
Provide a reservation form that captures team_name, coach_name, and contact_phone and submits a booking via submitFieldReservation.
Display a confirmation state using the response from submitFieldReservation, including the confirmed field, date, time, and status.

Support deep-linking so that URL parameters (date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly, reservationSlotId) restore the same filters and open the corresponding reservation form when revisiting or sharing the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Find and book an available field slot for a team practice
Steps: (1) User navigates to Fields & Reservations via header or footer navigation, optionally from index.html with pre-populated URL parameters (date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly). (2) On page load, the URL-state logic initializes filters from URL parameters and calls getFieldReservationFilterOptions to populate selectable dates, divisions, fields, and time windows. (3) The filters form selects an initial date (from URL or the first available date), division, field, time window, and lights-only flag. (4) The user adjusts any filters as needed (e.g., selects a date, division, time range, or toggles lights-only) and submits the filters. (5) The page calls searchFieldReservationSlots with the current filters and displays the resulting slots in a calendar/list component, clearly indicating which slots are available. (6) The user clicks an available slot, which triggers navigation to fields.html with the same filters in the URL plus reservationSlotId for the chosen slot. (7) On load, the slot details form calls getFieldReservationSlotDetails for that reservationSlotId and shows the slot summary above the form. (8) The user fills in team_name, coach_name, and contact_phone and submits the form. (9) The page calls submitFieldReservation. (10) If successful, the page shows a confirmation panel with confirmation_message and reserved_slot details, and optionally refreshes the slot list via searchFieldReservationSlots to reflect the newly booked status.

Workflow: Adjust filters after viewing slot details
Steps: (1) While a reservation form is open (reservationSlotId present in URL), the user decides to look for a different time or field. (2) The user closes the reservation form using a Close/Back control, which triggers URL update to remove reservationSlotId while retaining other filter parameters (date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly). (3) The URL-state manager detects the change, hides the reservation form, and leaves the current filter selections intact. (4) The user changes any filter and submits them again. (5) The page updates the URL parameters to match the new filters and calls searchFieldReservationSlots to refresh the slots list.

Workflow: Visit a deep-linked reservation form for a specific slot
Steps: (1) User opens a shared link such as fields.html?date=...&divisionId=...&reservationSlotId=.... (2) On page load, the URL-state manager reads date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly, and reservationSlotId. (3) The page first calls getFieldReservationFilterOptions to populate filter controls and selects the values matching the URL parameters where possible. (4) In parallel or immediately after filters are ready, searchFieldReservationSlots is called using the filter values from the URL to populate the slots list. (5) Because reservationSlotId is present, getFieldReservationSlotDetails is called to fetch detailed information about that slot and automatically open the reservation form. (6) If getFieldReservationSlotDetails indicates is_available is false, the form area instead shows an error/unavailable message and may prompt the user to adjust filters; the slots list remains available for selecting another slot, which updates reservationSlotId in the URL.

Workflow: Change only the date or time window while preserving other state
Steps: (1) User has already selected a set of filters and is viewing slots for a specific date. (2) The user changes the date or time window in the filter form. (3) When the user applies filters, the filter form updates the date and/or timeWindow parameters in the URL and clears reservationSlotId (if present) to close any open reservation form. (4) The URL-state manager responds to the updated URL, and the page calls searchFieldReservationSlots again with the new filters. (5) The slots list updates to show available slots for the newly selected date or time window while retaining division, field selection, and lights-only setting.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load and initial data fetch
Behavior: On fields.html load, the URL-state manager reads URL parameters (date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly, reservationSlotId). The page calls getFieldReservationFilterOptions once to obtain available_dates, divisions, fields, and time_windows. It then derives initial filter selections from URL parameters, falling back to valid options returned from getFieldReservationFilterOptions. Once filters are set, the page calls searchFieldReservationSlots with a filters object built from the selected date, division, field, time window, and lights-only flag.

Interaction: Filter change and search
Behavior: When the user changes any filter control (date picker, division selector, field selector, time window selector, lights-only toggle) and submits the filter form, the component updates the URL parameters to reflect current selections (adding or removing date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly). It also removes reservationSlotId from the URL to close any open reservation form. The page then calls searchFieldReservationSlots with filters corresponding to the URL parameters and updates the slots display.

Interaction: Click an available slot
Behavior: When the user clicks on an available slot in the slots list/calendar, the slots component triggers navigation to fields.html with all current filter parameters preserved and reservationSlotId set to the clicked slot's reservation_slot_id. After navigation (or history-state update), getFieldReservationSlotDetails is called with that reservationSlotId, and the reservation form opens populated with the slot details.

Interaction: Open reservation form via URL (deep link)
Behavior: If fields.html is loaded with reservationSlotId present in the URL, the page, after initializing filters and calling searchFieldReservationSlots, calls getFieldReservationSlotDetails for reservationSlotId. If the returned slot is available, its details are shown and the reservation form is enabled. If not, the form area displays an unavailable message and disables submission while leaving the slot list and filters usable.

Interaction: Submit reservation form
Behavior: When the user submits the reservation form, the form component validates that team_name, coach_name, and contact_phone are provided. If valid, it calls submitFieldReservation with reservationSlotId (from URL or form state) and the input values. On success, the component displays the confirmation_message and reserved_slot details returned by submitFieldReservation and may disable the form inputs. Optionally, it can trigger a refresh of the slots list via searchFieldReservationSlots so that the booked slot is no longer marked available.

Interaction: Close reservation form / back to slots
Behavior: When the user closes the reservation form (e.g., by clicking a Close or Back to available fields control), the reservation-form component triggers an update to the URL that removes reservationSlotId while preserving other filter parameters. The URL-state manager reacts by hiding the reservation form and keeping the current slots results intact.

Interaction: Breadcrumb navigation to Home
Behavior: When the user clicks the 'Home' crumb in the breadcrumb component, the page navigates to index.html (without adding or requiring parameters from this page).
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters fully define the user-visible state for filters and reservation selection on fields.html. On initial load and on every URL change, a URL-state manager parses the query string parameters and updates the page state accordingly:

- date (string): If present, the page attempts to match this ISO date to one of the available_dates returned by getFieldReservationFilterOptions. If a match is found, that date is preselected in the date picker and used in the filters passed to searchFieldReservationSlots. If absent or invalid, the page can select a fallback date using only dynamic data (for example, the first element in available_dates) without hardcoding any specific date.

- divisionId (string): If present, the page matches divisionId to a division_id from divisions returned by getFieldReservationFilterOptions. If matched, that division is preselected in the division filter and sent as part of the filters argument to searchFieldReservationSlots. If absent or not matched, the division filter is set to an 'all divisions' state, and no division-specific filter is applied when calling searchFieldReservationSlots.

- fieldId (string): If present, the page matches this to a field_id from fields in getFieldReservationFilterOptions. If matched, this field is preselected in the field filter and passed into searchFieldReservationSlots. If absent or unmatched, the field filter uses an 'all fields' state and omits a specific field constraint in filters.

- timeWindow (string): If present, it is expected to encode a start and end time (e.g., 'HH:MM-HH:MM'). The page parses this value and attempts to match it to one of the time_windows items (matching on start_time and end_time). If a match is found, that time window is selected in the filter UI, and those start and end times are included in filters passed to searchFieldReservationSlots. If absent or unmatched, the time window filter is set to an 'any time' state and is not included in filters.

- lightsOnly (boolean/string): If lightsOnly appears in the URL, the page interprets common truthy encodings (such as 'true' or '1') as enabling a 'lights only' filter. When enabled, the filter form sets its lights-only control to on and includes the corresponding constraint (for example, field_has_lights=true) in filters passed to searchFieldReservationSlots. If lightsOnly is absent or false, the lights-only filter is not applied.

- reservationSlotId (string): If present, the page opens the reservation form for that slot. After filters are initialized, getFieldReservationSlotDetails is called with reservationSlotId to populate slot details for the form. If reservationSlotId is removed from the URL (e.g., when the user closes the form or changes filters), the reservation form component hides itself. When the user clicks another available slot, the URL is updated with a new reservationSlotId, which in turn re-triggers getFieldReservationSlotDetails for the new slot.

All data required to render filters and slots is sourced dynamically from getFieldReservationFilterOptions, searchFieldReservationSlots, getFieldReservationSlotDetails, and submitFieldReservation. No field names, divisions, dates, or times are hardcoded; they are always taken from these interface responses. The URL is treated as the single source of truth for active filters and the selected slot, ensuring that using browser navigation (back/forward) or sharing a link reproduces the same state.

components.functionality: Displays a simple breadcrumb with a 'Home' link and a current-page label ('Fields & Reservations'). Provides navigation back to index.html when the Home crumb is clicked.

Central controller that parses URL parameters on page load and on history changes, initializes filter state and selected reservationSlotId, and notifies other components when URL-based state changes. It also provides utility methods for other components to update the URL when filters or selected slots change.

Fetches and renders available filter options (dates, divisions, fields, time windows) using getFieldReservationFilterOptions and allows the user to select filter criteria. On submission, it updates the URL parameters (date, divisionId, fieldId, timeWindow, lightsOnly) via the url-state-manager and triggers a new searchFieldReservationSlots call.

Displays the list or calendar view of field reservation slots returned by searchFieldReservationSlots according to the current filters. Each slot shows field_name, division_name, date, start_time, end_time, and field_has_lights indicators, and visually marks slots as available or unavailable based on is_available. Clicking an available slot requests a URL update to set reservationSlotId so that the reservation form opens for that slot.

When reservationSlotId is present in the URL, this component loads detailed information about that slot via getFieldReservationSlotDetails and displays the slot summary (field name, date, times, division, lights, notes). It renders a form for team_name, coach_name, and contact_phone. On submit, it calls submitFieldReservation and, on success, shows a confirmation state and can signal the url-state-manager or slots component to refresh availability.

Shows the outcome of a reservation submission by rendering the confirmation_message, status, and reserved_slot details returned by submitFieldReservation. It provides controls to either close the confirmation (which typically removes reservationSlotId from the URL) or leave the form context open for reference.

Displays contextual messages when searchFieldReservationSlots returns no slots for the current filters or when there is an error retrieving slots. It does not introduce any static domain data and simply reflects the dynamic conditions (e.g., zero-length slots list or error state).

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with full-bleed background. Content appears constrained to a centered max-width container with logo aligned to left columns and navigation aligned to right columns.
layout.layout_pattern: Within the global dark, full-width hero header (logo left, nav right) defined by the site shell, the Fields & Reservations page content begins as a centered, max-width column. Content follows a linear top-to-bottom stack of strong horizontal bands. First is a compact breadcrumb band at the top-left of the content area, then a bold, full-width filter strip acting as the functional 'hero' for this page. Below, a large, split layout section mirrors the mockup’s left-right hero split: reservation slots on the left and reservation details/confirmation on the right. Status and empty/error states are integrated into these bands rather than appearing as separate floating elements.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Within the global dark, full-width hero header (logo left, nav right) defined by the site shell, the Fields & Reservations page content begins as a centered, max-width column. Content follows a linear top-to-bottom stack of strong horizontal bands. First is a compact breadcrumb band at the top-left of the content area, then a bold, full-width filter strip acting as the functional 'hero' for this page. Below, a large, split layout section mirrors the mockup’s left-right hero split: reservation slots on the left and reservation details/confirmation on the right. Status and empty/error states are integrated into these bands rather than appearing as separate floating elements.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content is constrained to a central max-width container (approx. 1,200–1,320px) within a full-bleed dark background. The breadcrumb uses minimal height at the top. The filters strip occupies roughly 15–20% of the viewport height, spanning nearly the full width of the container. Below, the main reservation section occupies the majority of the remaining viewport height, divided horizontally: about 60–65% of the width for the slots results on the left and 35–40% for the details/confirmation panel on the right. Vertical gaps between bands and major components sit in the 40–64px range, preserving strong separation of zones while maintaining a bold, uncluttered look.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate-to-low. Each band focuses on a single primary task: navigation (breadcrumb), filtering, reviewing slots, then confirming a reservation. The filters strip has a few well-spaced controls rather than dense forms. The slots area shows time slots in clear rows or cards with generous vertical padding (16–24px) and consistent spacing between items. The right-hand details panel uses space to emphasize key text and buttons, avoiding tight stacking. Large fields of dark background color and clear separation lines maintain the bold, dramatic sports aesthetic without feeling crowded.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern adapted to this page: the eye starts at the breadcrumb in the top-left, then moves right along the filter strip where prominent action controls (e.g., 'Apply Filters' or 'Search') are right-emphasized. From there, the eye moves diagonally down to the upper-left of the main slots area, scanning down the list of available times. On selecting a slot, attention is pulled to the right-hand details and confirmation panel, echoing the mockup’s movement toward the right-aligned '.Closed' status. Strong contrast, all-caps headings, and right-weighted CTAs continue to guide users across and down the page.

layout.overall_description: Inside the dark, full-width site frame, the Fields & Reservations content sits in a centered, max-width container. At the very top of this content area, a slim breadcrumb trail anchors to the left, aligning with the logo’s left edge from the hero and subtly marking the user’s location. Below, a bold, full-width filters strip spans the container, like a functional echo of the registration strip from the mockup: a dark band with clear, left-aligned filter controls and a right-aligned primary filter action, all with generous horizontal padding. Beneath a comfortable vertical gap, the main reservation region opens up as a wide, split layout: on the left, a tall list of hourly field slots flows vertically down, each row or card separated by breathable space; on the right, a vertically centered, card-like details panel occupies about one-third of the width, ready to show the selected slot’s information and reservation form, with confirmation states appearing in the same zone. The overall effect preserves the mockup’s bold, high-contrast sports aesthetic and its strong left-to-right, top-to-bottom visual rhythm, while dedicating the most visual weight to the interactive reservation process.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation sits at the top of the page’s content area, directly beneath the global hero header, aligning with the left edge of the max-width content container. It occupies a single horizontal line of text, spanning roughly 70–80% of the container width but visually anchored to the top-left, leaving ample empty space to its right. Vertical padding above and below the breadcrumb is modest (about 8–16px), distinguishing it as a tertiary meta-navigation band without competing with the stronger bands below. The breadcrumb is left-aligned and uses lighter text on the dark background, forming a subtle entry point before the more dominant filter strip. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the breadcrumb and the top of the filters strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element participating in the normal document flow; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick to the top. It always sits as the first visible component in the content column beneath the global site header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager operates entirely behind the scenes. It has no visible footprint on the layout and does not claim any space in the grid. It simply coordinates state between the filter form, slots results, details form, and confirmation panel, ensuring that selections and filters are reflected in the URL. Visually, the layout behaves as if this component does not exist.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no rendered UI. It does not affect spatial behavior other than driving which of the visual components (results, details, confirmation, empty/error) are shown or hidden within their allocated areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation filters form appears as a dominant horizontal strip directly beneath the breadcrumb, spanning the full width of the central content container and visually echoing the registration strip from the mockup. The strip height is moderate—roughly 15–20% of the viewport on desktop—with internal vertical padding of 16–24px and horizontal padding of 24–32px. Filters (e.g., field selection, date, time range) are aligned primarily to the left half of this band, arranged in at most one to two rows to keep density low. Each control has 16–24px horizontal spacing from the next, preserving a clean rhythm. On the far right of the strip, aligned with the navigation feel from the mockup, a bold primary action (such as an APPLY FILTERS or SEARCH button) sits slightly isolated with extra right padding, drawing the eye across the band from left to right. The background of the strip is a solid dark or slightly darker band than the page background, with potentially a thin top or bottom rule to mark it as its own section. Below this band, a clear vertical gap of 40–48px separates it from the main slots/results area, reinforcing it as a distinct functional zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On larger viewports, the filters strip behaves as a normal block but may become sticky at the top of the content column when the user scrolls past it, anchoring just beneath the global header. This mirrors the strong, persistent presence of the registration strip in the mockup and ensures filters remain quickly accessible while browsing slots.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation slots results occupy the left portion of a large, horizontally split content section below the filters strip. This section spans the full width of the content container; the slots results take approximately 60–65% of that width, anchored to the left. There is a generous margin between the bottom of the filters strip and the top of the first slot row (~40–48px), allowing the eye to reset before diving into the list. Within this left column, slots are presented as vertically stacked rows or cards representing one-hour time blocks. Each row spans nearly the full width of the slots column, with 16–24px vertical padding and 16–24px spacing between rows. Text (time, field name, basic availability status) is left-aligned, mirroring the logo’s anchoring in the original hero, while any quick action affordance (e.g., a ‘Select’ button or icon) appears on the right side of each row—continuing the left-to-right scan pattern. The overall height of this slots area can extend beyond the viewport, and it scrolls vertically; as the user scrolls, this left column behaves like the main content stream. When there are many results, a comfortable bottom padding of 48–64px keeps the content from feeling cramped against the page bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Within the main split section, the slots results act as the scrolling column. On larger screens, they remain aligned to the left while the right-hand details/confirmation panel can be sticky, ensuring the selection context stays visible. On smaller viewports, this left column will expand to full width and the right panel will stack below, turning the layout into a vertically stacked series of sections while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation slot details form appears in a distinct right-hand panel within the same horizontal band as the slots results. This panel occupies roughly 35–40% of the content container width, aligned flush to the right edge, leaving a deliberate visual gap between the left and right segments that alludes to the large negative space in the mockup’s hero center. The vertical top edge of this panel aligns with the top of the first visible slot row to create a clear horizontal baseline. The panel has a card-like treatment: a slightly raised dark surface with internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. When no slot is selected, this panel may show a subtle prompt or remain visually subdued; when a slot is selected, the panel becomes visually active with a bold, all-caps heading (e.g., selected field and time) near the top, form fields arranged in a simple single-column layout down the center, and a strong primary action button aligned to the right at the bottom of the form, echoing the right-anchored navigational emphasis from the mockup. The form content uses generous vertical spacing (16–24px between fields) to maintain the low-to-moderate density. There is breathing room (at least 32–40px) between the bottom of this panel content and the section’s bottom edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, this panel is sticky relative to the viewport within the main content area: as the user scrolls through the list of slots on the left, the details panel remains pinned near the top-right (within its section), maintaining context for the selected slot. On narrow screens, this panel reflows below the slots list as a full-width section, losing the sticky behavior and stacking instead, but it maintains its generous internal padding and clear separation via top margin in the 40–48px range.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation confirmation panel is spatially bound to the same right-hand region as the slot details form, effectively taking over the panel’s interior once a reservation is successfully submitted. It does not occupy new global space; instead, it transitions within the existing right-side card area. When active, the panel fills the width of the right column, maintaining the 24–32px internal padding. The confirmation content is vertically centered or positioned slightly above center within the panel to create a sense of focus: a bold, all-caps confirmation heading appears at the top of this area, followed by key reservation details (field, date, time, team) in a compact vertical stack, and finally a secondary row of actions aligned toward the right (e.g., ‘New Reservation’, ‘View All Reservations’), echoing the mockup’s right-weighted status emphasis. The confirmation styling can draw inspiration from the red '.Closed' status in the mockup—perhaps a strong colored accent bar or badge near the top-right of this panel—while still harmonizing with the site’s dark aesthetic. Space between text blocks remains generous, keeping the confirmation easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The confirmation panel replaces or overlays the contents of the reservation-slot-details-form area without changing the overall panel dimensions or position. It scrolls only if the content exceeds the panel’s height but generally aims to fit above the fold on desktop. On small screens where the details panel stacks below the slots, the confirmation panel likewise appears as a full-width band in that same stacked position, pushing the underlying list down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty or error state for slots is displayed within the same left-hand region allocated to the reservation-slots-results, not as a separate overlay. When there are no results or an error occurs, the usual list of slot rows is replaced by a centered message block. This block sits in the middle of the left column both vertically (as space allows) and horizontally, with a maximum width of about 60% of the slots column, leaving ample dark negative space around it. A short, bold heading (e.g., NO AVAILABLE SLOTS) appears at the top of the block, followed by a smaller explanatory line and, if appropriate, a right-aligned inline action (such as ‘Clear Filters’) beneath. This mirrors the mockup’s use of strong typographic status messaging (like '.Closed') while keeping the message contained and easy to notice. The right-hand details panel remains visible but subdued—either showing a neutral placeholder state or encouraging the user to adjust filters—maintaining the page’s split layout even when there are no results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This state is conditional and fully replaces the slots list content in the left column while visible. It participates in the normal scroll flow of the slots area. On smaller screens where columns stack, the empty/error block is centered within the full-width slots section and continues to maintain generous top and bottom margins (40–64px) to distinguish it from the surrounding content.

name: Team Finder
title: Team Finder
description: Search and filter league teams by division, skill level, location, and practice schedule, then open team detail pages for more information.

core_features: Load available filter options for divisions, skill levels, practice days, and radius using getTeamFinderFilterOptions, and initialize the filter form based on URL parameters when present.
Allow users to filter teams by division, skill level, ZIP code, radius, and selected practice days via a search form.
Execute team search requests with searchTeams using the current filters, sort order, and pagination settings.
Display search results as a list of teams showing team name, division, skill level, distance (if available), ZIP code, and an abbreviated practice schedule derived from practice_summaries.
Provide sorting controls that adjust the searchTeams sort parameter and refresh the results.
Provide pagination controls that adjust page and page_size for searchTeams and update the results.
Support navigation to team_detail.html by clicking a team result row or a dedicated 'View Team' action, passing the team_id as teamId in the URL.
Maintain filter, sort, and pagination state in URL query parameters so Team Finder can be deep-linked or revisited from index.html with pre-populated criteria.
Offer a breadcrumb link back to index.html (Home) without preserving search state, as a simple navigation back to the main landing page.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open Team Finder from navigation with no preselected filters
1. User clicks 'Teams' in the site header or footer, navigating to teams.html with no query parameters.
2. The team-finder-controller component initializes and calls getTeamFinderFilterOptions to retrieve available divisions, skill levels, practice days, and radius options.
3. Once options are loaded, the team-filter-form is initialized with empty or default values (for example, no division selected, sort unset, page set to the first page).
4. The controller constructs a filters object (with any defaults applied) and calls searchTeams with filters, sort (if any), page set to the first page, and a default page_size.
5. While searchTeams is pending, a loading indicator is shown in the results area.
6. When searchTeams returns teams and total, the team-results-list renders the list of team rows and the pagination-controls show the current page and total pages based on total and page_size.

Workflow 2: Open Team Finder pre-filtered from index.html
1. On index.html, the user selects criteria (for example, a specific division, skill level, ZIP, radius, and practice days) and clicks a link or button that navigates to teams.html with corresponding URL parameters: divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays, and optionally sort.
2. On teams.html load, team-finder-controller parses the query parameters and calls getTeamFinderFilterOptions.
3. After filter options are received, team-filter-form is populated so that division, skill level, ZIP, radius, and practice day selections match the URL parameters.
4. The controller builds a filters object from the URL-derived form state and calls searchTeams with filters, sort (from the URL if present), and the first page.
5. The user immediately sees results aligned with their selections from index.html, with pagination and current filter values visible and adjustable.

Workflow 3: Refine results by practice days and location
1. The user views an initial set of results on teams.html.
2. In the team-filter-form, the user modifies filters: they may change division or skill level, update the ZIP code, change the radius selection, and add or remove practice days via a multi-select control.
3. The user submits the filter form (for example, clicks an 'Update Results' button).
4. team-filter-form emits an onSubmit event with the current form state to team-finder-controller.
5. The controller converts the form state into URL parameters: divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays (comma-separated), resets page to the first page, and updates the browser URL accordingly.
6. The controller constructs a filters object from the submitted values and calls searchTeams with filters, the current sort value, page set to the first page, and the current page_size.
7. The results list refreshes to show only teams that match the new filters, and the filter form continues to display the current selections.

Workflow 4: Change sort order of team results
1. The user sees a list of teams and wants to change the order (for example, by practice time or distance).
2. In the results-toolbar, the user selects a different sort option in a sort dropdown.
3. results-toolbar triggers an onSortChange event with the selected sort key.
4. team-finder-controller updates the sort parameter in the URL query string, resets page to the first page, and keeps existing filters and page_size.
5. The controller calls searchTeams with the unchanged filters, new sort value, page set to the first page, and current page_size.
6. The team-results-list re-renders with the teams in the new order.

Workflow 5: Navigate through multiple result pages
1. The user is viewing a list of teams and wants to see more results.
2. The user clicks a pagination control (for example, 'Next', 'Previous', or a specific page number) within pagination-controls.
3. pagination-controls emits an onPageChange event with the new page number.
4. team-finder-controller updates the page parameter in the URL (and keeps all other query parameters unchanged: filters, sort, page_size).
5. The controller calls searchTeams again with the same filters and sort, page set to the new page, and current page_size.
6. The results list updates to display the teams for the selected page; pagination-controls highlights the current page.
7. If the user copies the URL or refreshes the page, the same page of results is reloaded because page is preserved in the query string.

Workflow 6: Open a team detail page
1. In the team-results-list, each team row includes both a clickable row area and a specific 'View Team' action.
2. When the user clicks a row or the 'View Team' link, team-result-row emits an onClick event with the associated team_id from searchTeams.
3. The page navigates to team_detail.html with a teamId query parameter equal to the clicked team_id.
4. The user reviews full team details and can perform additional actions (such as saving the team) on the team_detail.html page.
5. Using the browser back button returns the user to teams.html with all filters, sort, and pagination state restored from the URL.

Workflow 7: Use breadcrumb to return to Home
1. The user is on teams.html and wants to return to the site home.
2. The user clicks the 'Home' breadcrumb link rendered by breadcrumb-nav.
3. The browser navigates to index.html (without passing search state by design).
4. The user can start a new flow, including launching Team Finder again with new or preselected filters.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load and initialization
- Trigger: Browser navigates to teams.html.
- System: team-finder-controller parses URL query parameters (divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays, sort, page, pageSize if present).
- System: Calls getTeamFinderFilterOptions to obtain selectable options for divisions, skill levels, practice days, and radius.
- System: After options load, initializes team-filter-form with values derived from the URL (or default values if parameters are absent) and then calls searchTeams with the corresponding filters, sort, page (defaulting to the first page when absent), and page_size (default when absent).
- UI: Shows a loading state until searchTeams returns, then renders results and pagination.

Interaction: Filter field change (without immediate search)
- Trigger: User changes an individual filter field (for example, division, skill level, ZIP, radius, or practice day selection) inside team-filter-form.
- UI: The form shows the updated value; a 'dirty' state can be tracked internally to indicate that filters differ from the last search.
- System: No immediate call to searchTeams is made until the user submits the form (to avoid excessive API calls), unless configured otherwise.

Interaction: Submit filter form
- Trigger: User submits team-filter-form.
- System: team-filter-form emits onSubmit with the current filter state.
- System: team-finder-controller transforms this state into URL query parameters: divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays (joined into a comma-separated string), preserves or sets sort, and resets page to the first page.
- System: The controller updates the browser URL query string to match the new filter and sort values.
- System: Constructs a filters object for searchTeams from the submitted values (including practiceDays parsed into the format expected by the API) and calls searchTeams with filters, sort, page set to the first page, and current page_size.
- UI: Displays a loading state in the results area, then re-renders team-results-list and pagination-controls using the new data.

Interaction: Sort change
- Trigger: User picks a different sort option from a dropdown in results-toolbar.
- System: results-toolbar emits onSortChange with a sort key.
- System: team-finder-controller updates the sort query parameter in the URL, resets page to the first page, and keeps existing filter parameters and page_size unchanged.
- System: Calls searchTeams with unchanged filters, new sort, page set to the first page, and current page_size.
- UI: Results re-render in the updated order.

Interaction: Pagination click
- Trigger: User clicks a page number or navigation control in pagination-controls.
- System: pagination-controls emits onPageChange with the target page number.
- System: team-finder-controller updates the page query parameter in the URL, keeping filters, sort, and page_size intact.
- System: Calls searchTeams with current filters, current sort, new page, and current page_size.
- UI: Results update to show the correct page, and pagination-controls highlights the new current page.

Interaction: Change page size (if exposed)
- Trigger: User selects a different number of results per page in results-toolbar (if implemented).
- System: results-toolbar emits onPageSizeChange with the new page_size.
- System: team-finder-controller sets the pageSize query parameter, resets page to the first page, and retains all other query parameters.
- System: Calls searchTeams with current filters, current sort, page set to the first page, and new page_size.
- UI: Results refresh showing a different number of teams per page; pagination recalculates based on total and page_size.

Interaction: Click a team result
- Trigger: User clicks a team row or its 'View Team' action in team-result-row.
- System: team-result-row emits onClick with the selected team_id.
- System: The page navigates to team_detail.html with teamId set to that team_id (for example, team_detail.html?teamId={team_id}).
- UI: Browser loads the team detail page; navigation back returns to the same search state because the URL on teams.html encodes it.

Interaction: Breadcrumb click
- Trigger: User clicks the 'Home' link in breadcrumb-nav.
- System: Navigates to index.html.
- UI: Team Finder page is left; any search state is abandoned unless index.html separately encodes or reuses some parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Team Finder page uses the browser URL query string as the single source of truth for search state. On initialization, the controller reads divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays, sort, page, and pageSize from the URL (if present). These values are used to pre-populate the filter form and control searchTeams calls. The practiceDays URL parameter is stored as a comma-separated string; when constructing the filters object for searchTeams, the controller parses this string back into the expected format (for example, an array or list) according to the API contract. If some parameters are absent, the controller applies internal default logic (such as first page index and a default page_size) without hard-coding any data values from the domain. Any user action that changes filters, sort, page, or page_size triggers an update of the corresponding URL parameters, ensuring that the current state is always reflected in the query string and allowing deep-linking and back/forward navigation to work seamlessly. The controller always derives the filters object passed to searchTeams from the current URL parameters and form state, not from hidden local storage or persistent client-side storage. Transient UI-only flags such as loading, error states, or whether filters are 'dirty' relative to the last search are maintained in in-memory component state and are not encoded in the URL. The results list and pagination are considered derived state from the last successful searchTeams response and the current page/page_size parameters.
