components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, calls to getTeamFinderFilterOptions and searchTeams, and synchronization between form state, URL parameters, and result rendering.
Displays a breadcrumb trail including 'Home' and 'Team Finder'; clicking 'Home' navigates back to index.html.
Provides input controls for division, skill level, ZIP code, radius, and practice days. Initializes its values from URL parameters and emits the combined filter state when submitted.
Specific sub-component of the filter form that renders multi-select options for practice days using practice_days from getTeamFinderFilterOptions and allows users to select one or more days.

Encapsulates the call to getTeamFinderFilterOptions and exposes the loaded options (divisions, skill_levels, practice_days, radius_options) to team-filter-form and results-toolbar, handling loading and error states for the options.
Displays the total number of teams returned by the last search, shows the current filters summary, and provides sort and page-size controls that emit changes back to the controller.

Renders the list of teams returned from searchTeams, including team name, division name, skill level, ZIP code, distance (if provided), and an abbreviated practice schedule built from practice_summaries for each team.

Represents a single team in the results list. Displays core team attributes and a concise representation of practice days and start times, and exposes a clickable area or action that navigates to the team detail page.
Shows controls for navigating between pages of search results (current page, total pages, next/previous). It uses the total count from searchTeams and the current page and page_size from URL state.

Wraps calls to searchTeams. Accepts filters, sort, page, and page_size from the controller, manages loading and error states, and provides the teams array and total to team-results-list, results-toolbar, and pagination-controls.
Displays the functional page title 'Team Finder' and may optionally display a concise subtitle explaining that users can search for teams by division, skill level, location, and practice schedule.
Displays a visual loading state when either filter options or team search results are being fetched, based on status flags exposed by filter-options-loader and search-results-loader.
Shows error feedback when getTeamFinderFilterOptions or searchTeams fails, and provides a control to retry the failed operation via the appropriate loader component.
Displays a message and simple guidance when searchTeams returns a total of zero teams, encouraging the user to broaden filters or change practice day or location criteria.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with full-bleed background. Content appears constrained to a centered max-width container with logo aligned to left columns and navigation aligned to right columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the site’s pattern of strong, full-width horizontal bands stacked vertically. Below the existing global hero/header, the Team Finder page begins with a full-width, dark hero band dedicated to orientation: breadcrumb on the upper left and a large page title on the left half, with the right half mostly negative space. Directly below, a shorter, full-width filter strip acts like the registration strip from the mockup: a dark band containing the team-filter-form laid out horizontally across the central content container. A thin horizontal rule underlines this strip, visually separating it from the results section. The results area is a single, centered column: results-toolbar at the top, then loaders/feedback states/results list, and pagination controls anchored at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the site’s pattern of strong, full-width horizontal bands stacked vertically. Below the existing global hero/header, the Team Finder page begins with a full-width, dark hero band dedicated to orientation: breadcrumb on the upper left and a large page title on the left half, with the right half mostly negative space. Directly below, a shorter, full-width filter strip acts like the registration strip from the mockup: a dark band containing the team-filter-form laid out horizontally across the central content container. A thin horizontal rule underlines this strip, visually separating it from the results section. The results area is a single, centered column: results-toolbar at the top, then loaders/feedback states/results list, and pagination controls anchored at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top hero band (breadcrumb + title) occupies roughly the upper 20–25% of the viewport height on typical desktop, with content constrained to a centered max-width (~70–80% of viewport width) inside a full-bleed dark background. Within this hero, the left 35–40% is used for breadcrumb and title, the center 30–40% remains mostly empty, and the right 20–25% is reserved as negative space, echoing the logo/nav split in the mockup. The filter strip below is shorter (about 10–15% of viewport height), but spans the full width; its internal content is aligned to the same centered container. The results section below consumes the remaining vertical space, stretching with content but preserving generous vertical gaps (32–64px) between the filter strip, toolbar, list, and pagination to maintain the low-density, bold feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays moderate-to-low: each major band focuses on one task—orientation (hero), filtering (filter strip), then exploration (results). Elements inside each band are given generous padding (16–24px inside blocks, 32–48px between bands). The filter form uses a small number of prominent inputs arranged in one or two rows rather than many small controls. Result rows are sizable, card-like bands with ample horizontal padding and breathing room between them, preventing the list from feeling cluttered despite containing multiple teams.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s Z-pattern: the eye first lands on the breadcrumb and bold page title in the top-left of the hero band, then scans horizontally across the open space to the right edge of the hero. From there, attention drops diagonally down to the center of the filter strip where the main filter controls are concentrated, then moves toward the right side of the filter strip where primary filter actions (e.g., submit/reset) reside. After filters are set, the gaze flows downward into the results-toolbar at the upper-left of the results band, then across to any right-aligned controls (e.g., sort). Finally, the eye traces down through the vertically stacked result rows and lands on the bottom-right pagination controls, echoing the strong left-to-right, top-to-bottom guidance from the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Team Finder page reads as a series of bold, horizontal bands stacked from top to bottom, each with a clear purpose. Immediately under the global site header, a tall, dark hero band introduces the page: a subtle breadcrumb sits near the top-left of the central content column, and beneath it a large, all-caps “TEAM FINDER” title anchors the composition. The left side of this band is heavy with typography while the center and right remain mostly open, preserving the dramatic negative space seen in the original mockup. Below this, a slightly shorter, full-width dark strip contains the filter form. Inputs are laid out in a single or double horizontal row across the center, with clear labels and a prominent action area toward the right, echoing the registration strip’s centered text and right-aligned status. A thin, bright horizontal rule runs full-width just beneath this strip, crisply marking the transition into the results area. The results section is a single centered column: at the top, a results-toolbar spans the width of the content, with the team count and key controls laid out left-to-right. Below that, the main body is either a clearly centered loader, a strong error/empty message panel, or a vertical stack of wide, card-like team rows aligned to the grid. Each card stretches across the container with robust padding and clear separation from the next. At the very bottom, pagination controls cluster toward the right edge of the content column, giving the list a defined endpoint. Throughout, the dark theme, bold color accents, and generous spacing create a high-impact, competitive sports feel while keeping the page legible and easy to scan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team-finder-controller acts as an invisible structural wrapper for the entire Team Finder content area. It begins immediately below the global site hero/header and extends down to just above the site footer, spanning 100% of the available width. Within this controller, content is constrained to a centered max-width container (aligned with the site’s 12-column grid), and all child components (hero band, filter strip, results section) are stacked vertically with consistent vertical spacing (32–64px) between them. The controller also manages any full-area overlays (such as the initial page loading-indicator), which are visually centered within this same content region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a normal block-level container in the document flow, but serves as the positioning context for page-level overlays (loading-indicator), which can be absolutely positioned relative to it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-nav is positioned at the top-left area of the Team Finder hero band, inside the centered max-width container. It sits approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the global header and aligns with the left edge of the content column (starting around the first or second grid column). It spans roughly 50–60% of the container width but remains visually compact, with text kept to a single line. There is a comfortable vertical gap (16–24px) between the breadcrumb-nav and the larger page-title-header below it, so the breadcrumb feels like a subtle orientation aid rather than competing with the main title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally at the top of the hero content stack; no sticky or fixed behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-title-header is the dominant element within the hero band. It is placed directly below the breadcrumb-nav, left-aligned to the same vertical grid line and occupying approximately the left 40–50% of the content container. The title uses large, all-caps typography with strong color contrast (e.g., bright team color against a dark background), extending over one or two lines. The area directly to its right, occupying the central 30–40% and rightmost 20–30% of the hero, is deliberately kept relatively empty, allowing the title block to stand out and echoing the mockup’s logo-left, empty-center, nav-right arrangement. The hero band provides generous vertical padding above and below the title (32–48px each), so the header feels anchored and powerful without being cramped by other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band; participates in normal document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team-filter-form occupies its own full-width horizontal strip directly beneath the hero band, visually reminiscent of the registration status strip from the mockup. This strip has a dark, slightly different tone from the hero to differentiate it, and spans the entire viewport width, while the form fields themselves are constrained to the same centered max-width container. Vertically, the strip is shorter than the hero—roughly 10–15% of the viewport height—with generous top and bottom padding (16–24px). Within the container, the filter controls are arranged horizontally in a single or two-row layout following the 12-column grid: primary filters (division, skill level, location) are placed starting from the left, each occupying 3–4 columns, with secondary filters (such as practice days) toward the right. Labels sit atop each input with 4–8px spacing. The rightmost area of the form (last 3–4 columns) is reserved for primary actions (e.g., a bold “Search” or “Apply Filters” button and a secondary “Reset” link), echoing the right-side emphasis of the '.Closed' status in the mockup. There is clear spacing (16–24px) between rows of inputs if a second row wraps on smaller widths, and the entire form band is separated from the results section below by a thin, full-width horizontal rule and an additional 24–32px of whitespace.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in normal document flow as a full-width section. On smaller screens, the internal layout collapses to a vertical stack while preserving the full-width strip treatment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The practice-day-multi-select is a distinct control within the team-filter-form, placed toward the right side of the filter grid to balance the visual weight of the left-aligned filters. On desktop, it typically occupies about 3–4 grid columns in the same row as other filters, with its label aligned to the top-left of its block and the input below. The control uses a pill or tag-based multi-select style that stretches most of the width of its assigned columns, maintaining 8–16px padding around selectable items for the bold, low-density feel. There is at least 16px of horizontal space separating this control from adjacent fields, so the multi-select doesn’t feel cramped. On mobile, it stacks below other filters with full-width layout, but still keeps generous vertical spacing (16–24px) from neighboring controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the team-filter-form and flows with that layout, wrapping to a new row when horizontal space is constrained.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-options-loader appears only when filter metadata (such as divisions or practice days) is being fetched. Visually, it overlays the content area of the team-filter-form without affecting the rest of the page. A semi-transparent dark scrim fills the entire filter strip region within the content container, while a compact loading spinner or progress indicator is centered both horizontally and vertically within this band. Existing labels and inputs beneath are subtly visible but clearly disabled, reinforcing the idea that the filter area is temporarily locked while options load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolutely positioned within the bounds of the team-filter-form’s container, covering the form area while active and removed entirely from the layout when inactive.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-toolbar marks the beginning of the results section and is positioned directly below the filter strip’s bottom rule, separated by 24–32px of whitespace. It resides inside the same centered max-width container and spans its full width, but its contents are laid out in a left-right split. On the left (first 6–8 columns), a line of text such as “X Teams Found” and possibly active filter chips are left-aligned, creating a strong anchor at the top of the results area. On the right (last 4–6 columns), sorting and view options are right-aligned, echoing the mockup’s right-side emphasis. The toolbar has a moderate height, with 12–16px of vertical padding above and below its contents. Its horizontal rule or subtle bottom border may mirror the thin white rule used below the filter strip, visually tying the bands together.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the toolbar becomes sticky within the results section: as the user scrolls, it pins to the top edge of the viewport within the content area, ensuring sorting and actions remain visible while the list scrolls beneath. On mobile, it behaves as a normal block in the flow to preserve vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team-results-list sits directly beneath the results-toolbar, with a 16–24px gap separating the two. It occupies the full width of the centered max-width container and extends downward as a vertical stack of team-result-row cards. The surrounding background remains dark, while each row card contrasts slightly (either darker or lighter) to stand out. The list has at least 24–32px of bottom padding before reaching the pagination-controls, giving the final row breathing room. When no results are present or when loading/errors occur, this list area is replaced visually by the appropriate feedback component, but the space reserved for it is still clearly within this results band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block container. The internal rows are stacked vertically and scroll with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each team-result-row is a wide, horizontally oriented card within the team-results-list. It stretches nearly the full width of the content container, leaving only the standard side padding from the main grid, and has generous internal padding on all sides (16–24px). The row’s content is organized into a 3-part horizontal layout: on the left (about 4 grid columns), the team name and division appear in bold, high-contrast text, drawing immediate attention. The center (about 4–5 columns) contains secondary details such as location, skill level, and typical practice days, arranged in two or three lines with comfortable line spacing. The right side (about 3–4 columns) is reserved for a strong call-to-action, such as a button labeled “View Team,” aligned to the right edge of the card and vertically centered. Rows are separated from one another by 16–24px of vertical space, and subtle separators or alternating background tones may be used to maintain a clear rhythm without feeling busy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list items within the team-results-list; they do not stick or float and scroll naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of the results section, following the last team-result-row and separated from it by a generous 32–48px vertical gap to signal the end of the list. The controls share the same centered max-width container, but the interactive elements (page numbers, previous/next) are grouped and right-aligned, occupying roughly the rightmost 4–6 grid columns. This mirrors the right-aligned status emphasis from the mockup and provides a strong visual endpoint on the bottom-right of the page. There is 24–32px of bottom padding beneath the controls before the page transitions into any footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows at the bottom of the results section in normal document order; no sticky behavior so that the footer remains visually distinct.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-results-loader appears within the results section between the results-toolbar and the team-results-list. When active, it replaces the list area visually: a loader (spinner or progress indicator) is centered horizontally in the content container and positioned roughly one-third down from the top of the visible results section, occupying at least 30–40% of the viewport height to give a sense of stage and importance. Surrounding the loader is ample negative space with no cards or other elements competing for attention, maintaining the low-density aesthetic. The toolbar above remains visible so users can see that they are viewing search results while the data loads.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the results section; it occupies the vertical space normally used by the team-results-list and is removed when loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator is used for full-page or major state transitions, such as the initial load of the Team Finder. It appears as an overlay centered within the entire team-finder-controller region, slightly dimming the hero, filter strip, and results area beneath with a semi-transparent dark scrim. A bold spinner or animated mark is positioned at the visual center of the viewport, not just the content container, ensuring it feels like a page-level state. Underlying content remains visible but is clearly disabled, reinforcing the high-contrast, dramatic sports aesthetic without clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed or absolutely positioned overlay spanning the full width and height of the team-finder-controller (and effectively the viewport). It sits above all other components and prevents interaction until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-message appears within the results section whenever fetching teams fails. It is positioned directly beneath the results-toolbar, in the same area normally reserved for the team-results-list or search-results-loader. The message block is centered within the content container and limited to about 60–70% of its width, keeping line lengths readable. It features a bold error icon or accent bar on the left and high-contrast text, possibly accented with the league’s red or team color to echo the mockup’s strong red '.Closed' status. Generous padding (24–32px) inside the message block and 24–32px of whitespace above and below keep it visually distinct yet integrated into the results band. No list items or pagination are shown when this message is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the team-results-list within the results section; it scrolls naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message occupies the same spatial zone as the team-results-list when a valid search returns no teams. It is placed beneath the results-toolbar, centered horizontally in the content container, and typically spans around 50–70% of the container width. The message and any supporting illustration or icon are vertically centered within the visible portion of the results band as much as possible, with at least 40–64px of padding above and below, creating an open, calm space. Typography remains bold and clear, but the color treatment is more neutral or slightly optimistic compared to the error state. No team-result-rows or pagination-controls appear when the empty state is visible, so the user’s focus remains on adjusting filters via the toolbar and filter strip above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the results section, replacing the team-results-list when active and scrolling with the page like any other content block.

name: Team Detail
title: Team Detail
description: Displays full details for a specific youth fastpitch softball team, including division, skill level, practice schedule, and coach contact information, and lets users save or remove the team from their My Teams list and return to the Team Finder results.

core_features: Load and display detailed team information (division, skill level, description, home/practice locations, practice days and times) using getTeamDetail based on the teamId URL parameter.
Show coach or manager contact details (name, email, phone) from getTeamDetail, clearly grouped for easy contact.
Allow the user to save the team to their personal My Teams list with an optional descriptive note using saveTeamToMyTeams.
Detect whether the current team is already in the user’s My Teams list using getMyTeams and reflect that state (e.g., show saved status and remove option).
Allow the user to remove this team from their My Teams list using removeSavedTeam.
Provide a conditional Back to Team Results link that returns the user to teams.html, preserving their original finder filters via finderParams when returnTo indicates navigation from Team Finder.
Provide a View My Teams navigation shortcut to user_dashboard.html with view=my_teams after a successful save.
user_workflows:
View team details from Team Finder:
1. User clicks a team in teams.html; the site navigates to team_detail.html with teamId, returnTo set to indicate Team Finder, and finderParams containing serialized filters.
2. On load, the page parses teamId, returnTo, and finderParams from the URL.
3. The page calls getTeamDetail with teamId and displays division, skill level, description, home field, zip code, coach contact details, and practice schedule.
4. The page calls getMyTeams to check if the current team is already saved and updates the Save/Remove controls accordingly.
5. User reviews the information and, if desired, uses the Save/Remove controls or the Back to Team Results link to return to teams.html with the original filters restored.

View team details from Schedule:
1. User clicks a team link from schedule.html; the site navigates to team_detail.html with teamId but without returnTo or finderParams.
2. On load, the page parses teamId from the URL.
3. The page calls getTeamDetail with teamId and displays all team information as above.
4. The page optionally calls getMyTeams to determine if the team is saved, showing Save/Remove controls.
5. Since returnTo and finderParams are not provided, the Back to Team Results link is hidden or disabled; the user can use the browser back button to return to the schedule.

Save a team to My Teams with an optional note:
1. User is viewing team_detail.html for a team that is not currently saved (determined via getMyTeams).
2. User clicks the Save to My Teams control.
3. A small form is shown allowing the user to enter an optional note describing why they are saving this team.
4. User submits the form; the page calls saveTeamToMyTeams with teamId taken from the URL and note taken from the form input.
5. While awaiting the response, the page shows a saving state and disables the Save action to prevent duplicate submissions.
6. On success (success=true in the response), the page updates local state to mark the team as saved, stores the returned saved_team_id, and refreshes the visual state (e.g., changing the control to Saved / Remove from My Teams).
7. A success message is shown with a View My Teams link that navigates to user_dashboard.html with view=my_teams.
8. Optionally, the page may call getMyTeams again to keep the full saved teams list in sync, if needed by other components.

Remove a team from My Teams:
1. User is viewing team_detail.html for a team that is already saved (determined by matching the current teamId against team entries returned from getMyTeams).
2. The Save area shows that the team is saved and provides a Remove from My Teams action.
3. User clicks Remove from My Teams.
4. The page calls removeSavedTeam with the corresponding savedTeamId (saved_team_id from getMyTeams or the previous saveTeamToMyTeams response).
5. While awaiting the response, the remove action is disabled and a removing state is shown.
6. On success, the page updates local state to mark the team as not saved, clears the savedTeamId for this team, and restores the Save to My Teams option.
7. Optionally, the page may call getMyTeams again to refresh the full My Teams list if needed.

Return to Team Finder results preserving filters:
1. User arrived at team_detail.html from teams.html with returnTo indicating Team Finder and finderParams containing serialized filter criteria.
2. The page parses finderParams into its constituent parameters (divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays) when initializing the Back to Team Results link.
3. The Back to Team Results link is configured to navigate to teams.html, passing divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, and practiceDays in the query string as extracted from finderParams.
4. User clicks Back to Team Results; the browser navigates to teams.html with those parameters.
5. teams.html reads those parameters to restore the user’s prior filter state and display the appropriate result set.

View My Teams after saving:
1. After a successful call to saveTeamToMyTeams, the page displays confirmation text including a View My Teams link.
2. User clicks View My Teams.
3. The browser navigates to user_dashboard.html with the URL parameter view set to my_teams.
4. The dashboard page is responsible for using getMyTeams to show the full list of saved teams; this is outside the scope of team_detail.html but the navigation is initiated here.
interactions: Page load with teamId parameter: triggers parsing of URL parameters (teamId, returnTo, finderParams), validation of teamId, an initial call to getTeamDetail(teamId), and an optional call to getMyTeams to determine saved state for this team.
Error handling for missing or invalid teamId: if no valid teamId is present in the URL, the page displays an error state explaining that no team could be loaded and does not attempt API calls.
Save to My Teams button click: opens or focuses the note entry form and prepares form submission using the current teamId and note value.

Save note form submit: validates that teamId is available, reads the note input (which may be empty), calls saveTeamToMyTeams(teamId, note), disables the submit control during the request, and updates the UI based on the response.

Remove from My Teams button click: calls removeSavedTeam(savedTeamId) for the saved team associated with the current teamId, disables the remove action while the request is pending, and, on success, updates the UI to show the team as unsaved.

Back to Team Results link click: if returnTo indicates navigation from Team Finder and finderParams are available, navigates to teams.html with extracted filter parameters in the query string; if conditions are not met, this control is not rendered or is disabled.
View My Teams link click: after a successful save, navigates to user_dashboard.html with view=my_teams in the query string.
Note input change: updates local component state with the user’s text so that the current value is used when submitting saveTeamToMyTeams.
Coach email click: opens the user’s default mail client using the email address from getTeamDetail (handled via a standard mailto link, not via an API).
Coach phone click or tap: on supported devices, initiates a call using the phone number from getTeamDetail (handled via a standard tel link, not via an API).
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and the responses of getTeamDetail, getMyTeams, saveTeamToMyTeams, and removeSavedTeam. URL parameter teamId is required; without it, the page shows an error and does not invoke APIs. On initial load, the page reads teamId, returnTo, and finderParams from the query string. teamId is passed directly to getTeamDetail to retrieve all display data for the team. In parallel or after that, getMyTeams is called with no parameters to obtain the current My Teams list. The page searches the returned teams array for an entry whose team_id matches the teamId parameter to determine if the team is saved and to retrieve its saved_team_id and note, if present. This information drives conditional rendering of Save vs Remove controls. If returnTo indicates that the user came from Team Finder and finderParams is present, finderParams is parsed into individual filter values (divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays) and stored in in-memory state for this page. Those values are used to construct the Back to Team Results navigation URL: teams.html with divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, and practiceDays as query parameters. The page itself does not modify finderParams or push new history entries; any state transitions (such as a successful save or removal) are reflected only in the UI state and, if needed, by re-calling getMyTeams. After saveTeamToMyTeams succeeds, the response's saved_team_id and saved_at are used to update local state so the team is marked as saved and removeSavedTeam can be called with the correct savedTeamId without requiring another full getMyTeams call. After removeSavedTeam succeeds, the page clears the local savedTeamId and saved flag for this team and may optionally re-synchronize via getMyTeams. Navigation to user_dashboard.html is controlled via a simple URL parameter view=my_teams and does not alter this page's state beyond unloading it.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, URL parameter parsing, API calls, and global state for the team detail view. It ensures that teamId is present, calls getTeamDetail to load team data, calls getMyTeams to determine saved status, and passes the resulting data and status flags to child components.

Displays the team’s core summary information (team name, division name, skill level, home field name, zip code, and short description) and may set the page-level heading based on team_name from getTeamDetail.

Shows the team’s recurring practice schedule using the practices array from getTeamDetail. Each list item displays the day_of_week, start_time, end_time, and field_name, allowing families to understand when and where practices occur.

Displays the coach or manager’s contact information, including coach_name, coach_email, and coach_phone from getTeamDetail. Provides mailto and tel actions where applicable so users can initiate contact outside the application.

Manages the Save to My Teams and Remove from My Teams behaviors for the current team. It determines whether the team is already saved by comparing the current teamId to entries returned by getMyTeams, shows an optional note field, calls saveTeamToMyTeams when the user saves (with teamId and note), and calls removeSavedTeam with the appropriate savedTeamId when the user removes. It updates the UI to reflect saving/removing states and exposes a flag to show the View My Teams link on successful save.

Displays confirmation and status after a successful saveTeamToMyTeams call. Shows the timestamp or confirmation text based on the saveTeamToMyTeams response and includes the View My Teams navigation link when appropriate.

Provides a Back to Team Results link when the user arrived from the Team Finder. It parses finderParams from the URL into individual filter parameters (divisionId, skillLevel, zip, radius, practiceDays) and constructs a navigation URL for teams.html that includes those values so the original search results can be restored.
Offers a View My Teams link that becomes available after a successful save operation. When clicked, it navigates to user_dashboard.html with the URL parameter view set to my_teams.

Handles and displays errors for this page, such as missing teamId, failure of getTeamDetail, or failures from saveTeamToMyTeams and removeSavedTeam. It informs the user of the issue and can suggest retrying or using navigation controls.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with full-bleed background. Content appears constrained to a centered max-width container with logo aligned to left columns and navigation aligned to right columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a strong top-to-bottom, banded layout that mirrors the mockup: a dominant full-width hero at the top, followed immediately by a full-width horizontal status/interaction strip, then a vertically stacked main content band that contains the team details (practice schedule and coach contact). The hero is split horizontally: team identity block anchored on the far left, navigation controls on the far right, with intentional empty space through the center. All subsequent content is arranged in single, full-width sections with inner two-column layouts where appropriate.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a strong top-to-bottom, banded layout that mirrors the mockup: a dominant full-width hero at the top, followed immediately by a full-width horizontal status/interaction strip, then a vertically stacked main content band that contains the team details (practice schedule and coach contact). The hero is split horizontally: team identity block anchored on the far left, navigation controls on the far right, with intentional empty space through the center. All subsequent content is arranged in single, full-width sections with inner two-column layouts where appropriate.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero section occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the initial viewport height, with the left ~25–30% reserved for the team summary header and the right ~20–25% for navigation controls, leaving ~45–55% as open negative space between them. Directly below, a shorter full-width strip (~10–15% of viewport height) is dedicated to the team save/status interaction, using a central alignment for the primary label and a right-aligned status message. The main content band beneath uses the remaining vertical space, with a left-dominant column for the practice schedule (~60–65% width) and a narrower right column for the coach contact panel (~35–40%). All sections are separated vertically by 48–64px to preserve the strong banded separation seen in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate-to-low. Each band focuses on a small number of elements, with generous padding (16–24px inside cards/panels and 24–32px around interactive controls) and wide gutters between columns. Navigation items are spaced apart rather than tightly clustered. The design emphasizes a bold, dramatic feel with large text blocks, clear sectional boundaries, and large areas of solid dark background that echo the mockup’s impactful, uncluttered aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern. The eye starts at the team name and division on the top-left hero area, moves horizontally to the top-right navigation controls (Back to Results and My Teams shortcut), then moves diagonally down toward the center of the page where the team save control strip sits. From the center of that strip, attention is pulled to the right-aligned save status or success message. From there, the eye continues down and back left into the main content area where the practice schedule list dominates, and finally across to the right-side coach contact panel. Error messages appear in a bold, central band just below the hero to intercept attention early while still respecting the overall vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: The Team Detail page presents as a dark, full-bleed, high-contrast layout with strong horizontal bands, echoing a competitive sports brand. At the very top, a tall hero block stretches edge-to-edge; on its far left, the team summary header forms a solid, structured column occupying about a quarter of the page width, featuring the team name and division in bold, all-caps typography. On the far right of this same band, two navigation links stack vertically, spaced comfortably and aligned to the right edge, while the center of the hero remains largely empty, emphasizing the powerful contrast between left and right anchors. Immediately beneath, a full-width, darker strip runs horizontally, functioning as the team save/status area: a centered line of text or control inviting users to save/remove the team and, toward the right side of the strip, a prominent textual status such as confirmation of a successful save. A thin, high-contrast horizontal rule underlines this strip and marks the transition into the main content. Below this rule, the content resolves into a wide single section with an inner two-column layout: on the left, a stacked list of practice schedule entries fills most of the width, and on the right, a more compact but visually solid card holds coach contact details. Vertical spacing between these bands is generous, creating distinct stripes of content separated by dark negative space. When errors occur, a vivid, full-width message panel appears just under the hero, pushing down the rest of the content but maintaining the same banded, high-impact structure. The overall effect is a bold, organized layout that guides the eye in a clear Z-pattern from brand identity, to navigation, to key status actions, and finally into detailed schedule and contact information.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full-bleed dark background spanning 100% of the viewport width, with a centered max-width content container (approximately 1200–1440px) aligned to an implied 12-column grid. All components are arranged in stacked horizontal bands inside this container: the hero band (team summary + top-right navigation) at the top, followed immediately by the full-width team save/status strip, then the main content band that houses the practice schedule on the left and coach contact panel on the right. Vertical spacing between these major bands is 48–64px, maintaining the strong sectional separation from the mockup. On smaller screens, the controller collapses the inner layouts to single-column stacks while preserving the same band order and generous spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a static, scrollable wrapper; no fixed or sticky behavior, but sets the global grid, max-width container, and sectional vertical rhythm for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team summary header is positioned in the upper-left portion of the hero band, aligned flush with the left edge of the max-width container. It occupies roughly 25–30% of the page width on desktop, spanning from the top of the hero down toward its vertical center, with substantial internal padding (24px all around). Within this block, the team name appears as a large, bold, all-caps headline, with division and skill level stacked beneath in smaller but still assertive text. The right edge of this block aligns roughly with the 3rd–4th column of the grid, leaving a wide swath of intentional empty space between it and the right-aligned navigation controls, echoing the mockup’s expansive middle area. On smaller screens, this header stretches closer to full width and is stacked above the navigation controls while retaining strong left alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero; no sticky behavior. The block maintains its left alignment and proportional width but shrinks responsively to fit smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The practice schedule list sits in the main content band directly beneath the team save/status strip and its dividing horizontal rule. Within the max-width container, it is anchored to the left, occupying approximately 60–65% of the width on desktop (roughly columns 1–7 or 1–8). The list appears as a vertical stack of schedule items, each rendered as a card-like row with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between items. The top edge of the first schedule card aligns visually with the top of the coach contact panel to its right, creating a cohesive horizontal line. On smaller screens, the practice schedule list expands to near-full width and appears above the coach contact panel, retaining generous spacing and clear separation between entries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content band, adapting from a left-dominant column on larger screens to full-width stacked content on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coach contact panel is placed to the right of the practice schedule list within the same main content band, aligned with the right edge of the max-width container. It occupies about 35–40% of the width on desktop (roughly columns 9–12), separated from the schedule list by a clear gutter of 24–32px. The panel is card-like with 20–24px internal padding and a solid dark background, featuring the coach’s name, role, and contact information stacked vertically. Its top aligns with the topmost practice schedule item to form a unified horizontal row, but it typically extends less vertically, creating a strong, compact right-side block. On smaller screens, this panel drops below the practice schedule list and stretches toward full width while maintaining the same padding and visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned within the main content band; behaves as a right column on larger viewports and a full-width block below the schedule list on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team save control occupies the central portion of the full-width status strip immediately beneath the hero band. The strip itself spans edge-to-edge with a deep black or very dark background, and the control is horizontally centered within the max-width container. The control presents a short, all-caps label (e.g., “SAVE THIS TEAM”) and a prominent button or toggle, arranged in a single line or tight horizontal cluster, with 16–20px vertical padding above and below and 24–32px horizontal padding around the interactive element. This central placement mirrors the mockup’s centered “2026 Spring Registration” heading and ensures the save action is perceived as a key functional state of the team. The save control’s vertical height, combined with generous padding, makes the entire strip around 10–15% of the viewport height on initial load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the status strip; remains centered and full-width responsive, stacking its label and button vertically on very narrow screens while staying in this dedicated band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save success message shares the same horizontal status strip as the team save control but is visually anchored toward the right side of the max-width container. When present, it appears on the same horizontal line or just beneath the save control, aligned to the right edge, occupying roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the width. The text is rendered in bold, high-contrast color (e.g., bright green or league red) and slightly larger than body text, echoing the prominence of the '.Closed' status in the mockup. A small horizontal offset (16–24px) separates it from the main save control cluster, reinforcing the idea that this is a status readout responding to the central action. On smaller screens, the message drops below the save control, still aligned right within the strip, with extra vertical spacing (8–16px) to keep the band readable and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual and conditional within the status strip: it appears or disappears based on state but always occupies the right side of the strip, flowing beneath the save control on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Back to Results navigation control sits in the hero band on the far right, forming the top line of a two-row navigation stack that mirrors the mockup’s navigation pattern. It is right-aligned within the max-width container, occupying about the rightmost 20–25% of the width, and positioned vertically near the top of the hero so it’s immediately visible. The link is styled as bold, uppercase text with a clear hit area and about 12–16px vertical padding, separated from the My Teams shortcut beneath by 8–12px of vertical space. This placement draws the eye horizontally from the team summary header across to the right, fulfilling the first leg of the Z-pattern and making it easy to return to search results at any time. On smaller screens, it remains right-aligned but may move closer to the top edge of the stacked hero header, preserving hierarchy over the My Teams link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero band, right-aligned. It does not stick on scroll, but remains consistently positioned in the top-right region across breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The My Teams shortcut link is positioned directly beneath the Back to Results navigation within the hero’s right-hand column, completing the two-line navigation stack seen in the mockup. It shares the same right alignment and width (rightmost 20–25% of the container) but sits slightly lower, with an 8–12px vertical gap that keeps the items visually separate. The link is rendered in all caps and may use a high-contrast accent color (e.g., league yellow or red) to emphasize it as a key action, similar to the highlighted 'REGISTER' item in the mockup. This stacked pairing of links forms a compact navigation block that balances the weight of the team summary header on the left, with generous negative space between them. On narrow screens, the My Teams link stays beneath the Back to Results link in a right-aligned vertical stack just below the team summary header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned in the hero band beneath the Back to Results control, with responsive stacking but consistent relational positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The team detail error state occupies a full-width band just below the hero section and, when active, pushes down the save/status strip and main content. It spans the entire max-width container and aligns with its left and right edges, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides. The message is centered horizontally, rendered in bold, high-contrast text (often red on a very dark background), and may include a short explanation plus a retry or back link. The vertical height of this band is similar to or slightly taller than the save/status strip, clearly distinguishing it as a disruptive state. When present, it becomes the primary focal point after the hero, intercepting the Z-pattern flow directly under the team summary, while still preserving the overall banded vertical structure. On smaller screens, the message text wraps but remains centered and generously spaced to avoid visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally inserted as a full-width horizontal band between the hero and the save/status strip. It is static and scrolls with the page, replacing or obscuring subsequent content only visually by pushing it downward.

name: Rules & Policies
title: Rules & Policies
description: Browse, filter, read, and bookmark league rules and policies by division and category, with direct access to specific documents via URL parameters.

core_features: Load available division and category filters using getRuleFilterOptions, and apply any incoming division or category URL parameters to pre-select filters.
List rule and policy documents using listRuleDocuments with the current filters and pagination, allowing users to click a document to view its full content.
Open and display an individual rule or policy document in a detail view using getRuleDocumentDetails based on ruleId (or resolved from articleId).
Support deep-linking to specific documents via articleId in the URL by resolving the matching rule document and loading its details.
Allow users to save or bookmark rule documents to their My Resources list using saveRuleDocumentToMyResources, and reflect saved state in the UI.
Load the user’s saved rule documents using getMyResources to show a My Resources summary and indicate which documents are already saved.
Allow users to remove saved resources using removeSavedResource from within a My Resources summary section.
Provide navigation from the Rules & Policies page to Home (index.html) and to the user dashboard My Resources view (user_dashboard.html?view=my_resources).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Browse rules by division and category
1. User navigates to rules.html from the header or footer without specific URL parameters.
2. Page controller calls getRuleFilterOptions to retrieve available divisions and categories.
3. The filter bar initializes its selected division and category based on URL parameters if present; otherwise it uses defaults derived from the getRuleFilterOptions response.
4. Page controller calls listRuleDocuments with filters reflecting the selected division and category and an initial page/page_size.
5. The document list displays the returned documents and total count.
6. User adjusts division or category in the filter bar.
7. Filter bar updates the URL parameters (division and/or category) and notifies the page controller.
8. Page controller calls listRuleDocuments again with the updated filters and resets pagination.
9. The list updates to show documents matching the new filters.

Workflow 2: Open a document from the list
1. User is on rules.html with a list of documents loaded.
2. User clicks a document title in the list.
3. The click handler navigates to rules.html with ruleId set to the clicked document’s rule_document_id, preserving current division and category parameters.
4. On load (or URL change), the page controller detects ruleId and calls getRuleDocumentDetails with that ruleDocumentId.
5. The detail panel displays the document title, metadata, last_updated, and full content.
6. The list remains visible (if designed that way), highlighting the selected document based on ruleId.

Workflow 3: Deep-link to a document via articleId
1. User clicks a link elsewhere (e.g., footer or dashboard) that navigates to rules.html with articleId set (and optionally division or category).
2. On load, the page controller reads articleId from the URL.
3. Page controller calls getRuleFilterOptions to populate filters. If division or category URL parameters are present, they are used; otherwise filters remain broad enough to include the articleId.
4. Page controller calls listRuleDocuments with filters that include articleId (plus any division/category filters derived from the URL, if provided).
5. From the listRuleDocuments response, the page controller identifies the first document whose article_id matches the URL articleId.
6. If a matching document is found, the page controller calls getRuleDocumentDetails using its rule_document_id and updates the URL to include ruleId for that document (using history/navigation) while retaining articleId.
7. The detail panel displays the document, and the filter bar reflects the document’s division_code and category (if desired, the filters may be updated to match the document’s attributes and the URL updated accordingly).

Workflow 4: Save a document to My Resources from the detail view
1. User has a document open in the detail panel (ruleId is present in the URL and details are loaded via getRuleDocumentDetails).
2. Page controller ensures current saved resources are loaded by calling getMyResources (if not already loaded).
3. The detail panel compares the current rule_document_id with the list from getMyResources to determine whether it is already saved.
4. If not saved, the detail panel shows a control labeled to save/bookmark the document.
5. User activates the save control.
6. Detail panel calls saveRuleDocumentToMyResources with the current rule_document_id.
7. On success, the page controller optionally calls getMyResources again or locally merges the new saved resource into its saved resources state.
8. The detail panel updates its UI to indicate the document is saved (e.g., toggled button state) and reveals a link/button to "View My Resources".
9. User may click "View My Resources" which navigates to user_dashboard.html with view=my_resources.

Workflow 5: View and manage saved resources from within rules.html
1. User visits rules.html; page controller calls getMyResources during initialization (either immediately or after the first successful save) to load the current saved resources.
2. My Resources summary component displays the total number of saved resources and may list a subset of them.
3. User clicks a saved resource in the summary list (if displayed).
4. Click handler navigates to rules.html with ruleId set to that resource’s rule_document_id, preserving existing filter parameters when appropriate.
5. Detail panel loads the document via getRuleDocumentDetails and indicates it is already saved.
6. If an unsave/remove option is provided in the summary, user can click a remove control next to a saved resource.
7. My Resources summary calls removeSavedResource with the saved_resource_id.
8. On success, the summary updates its list and total count, and the detail panel, if currently showing that document, updates its saved/unsaved state accordingly.
interactions: On initial page load or when URL parameters change, the page controller reads division, category, articleId, and ruleId from the URL and coordinates calls to getRuleFilterOptions, listRuleDocuments, getRuleDocumentDetails, getMyResources as needed.

When the user changes the division filter in the filter bar, the component updates the division URL parameter, clears or adjusts ruleId and articleId if necessary, and triggers a new call to listRuleDocuments with the updated division filter and reset pagination.

When the user changes the category filter, the component updates the category URL parameter and triggers a new call to listRuleDocuments with the updated category filter and reset pagination; if a currently open document no longer matches the filters, the detail view can be cleared or left unchanged based on design rules.

When the user navigates between pages of results in the document list, the pagination control updates the page number (which may be optionally reflected in the URL) and triggers listRuleDocuments with the new page value and existing filters.

When the user clicks a document in the list, the list component navigates to rules.html with ruleId set to that document’s rule_document_id (and division/category preserved), causing the page controller to call getRuleDocumentDetails for that document.

When the user opens rules.html with only an articleId parameter, the page controller uses listRuleDocuments with filters including the articleId to locate the corresponding rule_document_id, then calls getRuleDocumentDetails and updates the URL to include ruleId for stable navigation.

When the user clicks the save/bookmark control in the document detail view, the detail component calls saveRuleDocumentToMyResources; on success, it updates the local saved state and may trigger getMyResources to refresh the My Resources summary.

When the user clicks an unsave/remove control in the My Resources summary, the summary component calls removeSavedResource and, upon success, updates its list, total count, and notifies the detail view if it is displaying the same resource.
When the user clicks "View My Resources" in the detail or summary components, the page navigates to user_dashboard.html with view set to my_resources without additional API calls from this page.
When the user clicks the Home item in the breadcrumb navigation, the page navigates to index.html, leaving rules.html and its state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the primary source of truth for the Rules & Policies page state. On load, the page controller reads division, category, articleId, and ruleId from rules.html query parameters.

Division and category
- If division is present in the URL, it is used as the selected division in the filter bar after getRuleFilterOptions returns available options. If it is absent, the filter bar selects a default division based on the filter options (for example, a league-wide or first-listed option) without hardcoding a specific value.
- Similarly, if category is present in the URL, it is used as the selected category; otherwise the filter bar uses a default derived from getRuleFilterOptions.
- Whenever the user changes division or category, the filter bar updates the URL with the new values using navigation/history and notifies the page controller. This causes a new call to listRuleDocuments using filters that reflect the updated division and category. Pagination is reset when filters change.
- Optionally, a page parameter can be maintained in the URL to persist the current page across reloads; if used, it is always interpreted in conjunction with the current division/category filters.

ruleId and articleId
- ruleId in the URL always takes precedence for selecting the currently open document in the detail view. If ruleId is present, the page controller calls getRuleDocumentDetails with that ID, regardless of articleId, as long as the detail view is enabled.
- articleId is interpreted as a higher-level identifier used when ruleId is not provided or when resolving a deep link. If articleId is present but ruleId is absent, the page controller calls listRuleDocuments with filters that include articleId plus any division/category parameters. From the returned documents, a document with a matching article_id is selected, and its rule_document_id is used in a subsequent getRuleDocumentDetails call.
- After resolving articleId into a specific rule_document_id, the page controller updates the URL to include ruleId corresponding to that document. articleId may remain in the URL for traceability but is no longer needed to fetch the content.
- When filters (division/category) change such that the current ruleId no longer reflects a document in the current list, behavior can be defined as either: (a) preserve the detail view as-is, or (b) clear ruleId from the URL and hide the detail view. In either case, the filter change updates the URL and triggers a reload of listRuleDocuments.

Saved resources
- The saved state of documents (whether a document is in My Resources) is not encoded in URL parameters but is derived from getMyResources.
- On initialization, the page controller may call getMyResources to populate an in-memory map from rule_document_id to saved_resource_id. This is used by the document list and detail view to indicate saved status.
- When saveRuleDocumentToMyResources or removeSavedResource are called, the controller updates this in-memory state (and optionally re-calls getMyResources) without altering URL parameters.

Navigation and history
- Clicking a document in the list or a saved resource in the My Resources summary always results in navigation to rules.html with ruleId set appropriately, and with existing division/category parameters preserved or updated to match the document, depending on chosen behavior.
- Clicking Home in the breadcrumb navigates to index.html and clears all rules.html-related state.
- Clicking "View My Resources" navigates to user_dashboard.html with view=my_resources, relying on the dashboard page to interpret that parameter.

No use of localStorage or other client-side persistence is required; all persistent aspects of page state are represented via URL parameters and derived API responses.

components.functionality: Central coordinator for the Rules & Policies page. On load and on URL changes, it reads division, category, articleId, and ruleId parameters, calls getRuleFilterOptions to populate filters, calls listRuleDocuments with the current filters and pagination, resolves articleId to a specific rule_document_id when needed, calls getRuleDocumentDetails for the selected rule, and manages loading of saved resources using getMyResources. It shares derived state (selected filters, document list, selected document details, saved resources map) with child components.
Displays a simple breadcrumb path (e.g., Home > Rules & Policies). Provides navigation back to the home page when the Home item is clicked.
data_binding: URLParams (no dynamic data from APIs required)

components.functionality: Renders controls to select division and category filters for rule documents. It populates its options from getRuleFilterOptions, initializes selections from URL parameters or defaults, and notifies the page controller whenever the user changes a filter. It updates the URL parameters for division and category to keep state shareable and deep-linkable.

Displays a list of rule and policy documents returned from listRuleDocuments based on the current division and category filters and pagination. Shows basic metadata for each document (such as title, division_code, category, last_updated). Each list item is clickable to open the document detail by navigating to rules.html with ruleId set. The component also exposes pagination controls that adjust the page parameter used in listRuleDocuments.
data_binding: SavedResource (via getMyResources, to indicate which list items are saved)

components.functionality: Shows the full content and metadata of the currently selected rule or policy document. It is populated using getRuleDocumentDetails based on ruleId resolved by the page controller. The component also displays a save/bookmark control whose state reflects whether the document is in My Resources (determined by comparing the current rule_document_id with data from getMyResources). It initiates saveRuleDocumentToMyResources when the user saves a document and may allow unsaving by triggering removeSavedResource if the saved_resource_id is known. It also displays a "View My Resources" link/button once a document is saved.

Provides a compact overview of the user’s saved rule documents. It loads data using getMyResources, displays the total count, and may show a subset of saved items. Clicking a saved item navigates back to rules.html with ruleId set to that item’s rule_document_id. Each item may include a remove control that calls removeSavedResource. The component also shows a "View My Resources" action that navigates to user_dashboard.html with view=my_resources.
data_binding: RuleDocument (indirectly via titles and metadata in getMyResources response)

components.functionality: Manages pagination state for the document list. It tracks the current page and page_size (from URL or defaults), provides next/previous and specific page selection controls, and triggers listRuleDocuments via the page controller when the page changes. It may optionally synchronize the current page number with a URL parameter so that pagination is preserved on reload and shareable links.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with full-bleed background. Content appears constrained to a centered max-width container with logo aligned to left columns and navigation aligned to right columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page continues the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a strong, bold page header band for the Rules & Policies context, immediately followed by a full-width filter/status strip, then a large main content band where the rule list and detail view share a split layout, and finally a full-width personal resources strip. Within the main band, the mockup’s hero split is echoed: a dominant left block (rule list), a clear central gutter of negative space, and a right-hand block (rule detail). All components align to a centered max-width container resting on a 12-column grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page continues the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a strong, bold page header band for the Rules & Policies context, immediately followed by a full-width filter/status strip, then a large main content band where the rule list and detail view share a split layout, and finally a full-width personal resources strip. Within the main band, the mockup’s hero split is echoed: a dominant left block (rule list), a clear central gutter of negative space, and a right-hand block (rule detail). All components align to a centered max-width container resting on a 12-column grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper third of the viewport is devoted to page-level orientation: page controller, breadcrumb, and a bold horizontal filter bar that reads like a functional cousin of the registration strip. The middle of the page (roughly the next 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load) is dominated by the two-part content band: the rule-document-list on the left occupying about 55–60% of the content width (7 of 12 columns), a generous vertical gutter of 24–32px, and the rule-detail-panel on the right using about 40–45% (5 of 12 columns). The lower portion of the page is reserved for personal and navigational utilities: pagination closely attached to the list, and a strong, slightly shorter full-width strip for the my-resources-summary that mirrors the registration bar’s presence without overpowering the main content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderate-to-low to preserve the bold, dramatic feel: few elements per band, clear separation of sections (40–64px vertical margins), and breathing room around each control. The filter bar presents only the most essential inputs in a single horizontal band. The rule list favors legible, card-like rows with 16–24px vertical spacing instead of tight tables. The detail panel uses generous internal padding and typographic hierarchy rather than dense, small text. Overall, large color fields and clean blocks dominate over busy UI.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a repeated Z-pattern derived from the mockup. The eye lands first on the page title in the top-left, then moves to any key page-level action in the top-right. From there, it travels diagonally down to the left side of the filter bar, scanning filters horizontally toward the right where a bold result/status readout sits, echoing the '.Closed' emphasis from the mockup. The gaze then moves diagonally down again into the rule-document-list at the lower left, and is finally pulled across to the rule-detail-panel on the right, which acts as the endpoint of each scan. The my-resources-summary strip at the bottom forms a final, full-width horizontal anchor that closes this flow and signals the end of the page’s core interaction area.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s global hero and the thin white rule that ends the registration strip, the Rules & Policies page opens with a bold, dark page header band centered in the viewport. The title and high-impact typography anchor the top-left of the content container while an optional primary action sits on the far right, separated by a wide field of negative space, echoing the logo-nav split from the mockup. Immediately beneath, a subtle breadcrumb tucks into the left edge, and after a comfortable gap the layout drops into a full-width, dark filter bar that reads like a slimmer version of the registration strip: key filters line up from left to center, while a bright, high-contrast results/status readout on the right mimics the visual weight of the '.Closed' label. Below this, the main content band fills most of the remaining viewport height on initial load. It is divided into two asymmetrical columns: a taller, scrolling list of rule documents on the left and a visually solid rule detail panel on the right, separated by a clear vertical gutter and plenty of breathing room. The detail panel feels like a fixed anchor as the list scrolls, reinforcing the Z-shaped reading path as the user scans from list item titles across to detailed content. Beneath the list, centered pagination controls conclude the browsing band. Finally, a dark, full-width strip for saved resources spans edge-to-edge across the bottom, with center-weighted text and a right-aligned count or action, neatly mirroring the structural rhythm of the registration bar and providing a strong, confident footer-like closure to the page’s primary interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rules-page-controller occupies the first content band directly below the site’s global header and its terminating white rule. It spans the full width of the centered max-width container (roughly 80–90% of the viewport width on desktop) while respecting the 12-column grid. Vertically, it uses about 15–20% of the initial viewport height, with a top margin of 48–64px from the header edge to maintain the mockup’s generous separation. Within this band, the main 'RULES & POLICIES' title block is anchored to the left, spanning approximately the left 6–7 columns (about 55–60% of the content width). The title is set in bold, all-caps typography with a short, one-line description immediately beneath it, both left-aligned. On the far right, aligned to the rightmost 3–4 columns, sits an optional primary action (for example, a 'DOWNLOAD FULL RULEBOOK' button) or contextual control. A wide expanse of negative space separates the title block from this right-side control, mimicking the hero’s logo-left, nav-right, empty-center composition. The bottom edge of the page-controller band has a 24–32px gap before the breadcrumb, keeping this header visually isolated as a distinct, brand-forward section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the top of the page’s content area; scrolls normally with the page, acting as a structural and logical parent for the other components but with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rules-breadcrumb sits immediately beneath the page-controller band, aligned to the same left grid edge for continuity. It occupies the full width of the content container but uses only the leftmost 4–6 columns for the actual breadcrumb links, leaving the remaining space open to preserve the light, uncluttered feel. Vertically, it forms a slim band of about 32–40px height, with 8–16px of top padding so the breadcrumb text has breathing room above and below. The breadcrumbs are left-aligned, in smaller, lighter-weight text that clearly reads as tertiary navigation. The right side of this band remains visually quiet and empty, ensuring the breadcrumb does not compete with the bold title above or the filter bar below. A subtle divider (thin, low-contrast line) can sit at the bottom of this area, or a 16–24px gap is left before the filter bar, establishing a clear yet understated separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls away normally; it does not stick to the viewport, reinforcing its role as lightweight context rather than a persistent control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rules-filter-bar presents as a full-width, high-contrast horizontal strip stretching across the entire content container, visually similar to a slightly shorter version of the registration strip from the mockup. It begins roughly 16–24px below the breadcrumb, creating a clear banded separation. The bar’s background is a deep, solid dark shade, giving it a decisive presence. Vertically, it occupies about 10–15% of the viewport height on desktop or a fixed height of roughly 72–96px, with internal padding of 16–24px all around. Inside, the layout follows a left-to-right emphasis: key filter controls (such as division dropdown, category selector, search field, and a 'Bookmarked only' toggle) are aligned horizontally starting from the left, generally filling the left 7–8 columns with ample spacing between each control (at least 16–24px gaps). These controls are grouped to form a cohesive cluster rather than spanning edge to edge, maintaining moderate density. On the far right, occupying the last 3–4 columns and right-aligned, a bold status/summary text—such as '34 RULES • 12U DIVISION'—is rendered in an accent color (e.g., red or yellow) and slightly larger size, directly echoing the visual emphasis of the '.Closed' text from the mockup. This right-anchored summary becomes the visual endpoint for the eye when scanning the bar. The bottom of the bar is clean and may have a thin white or light line to mark the transition to the main content band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the top of the viewport once scrolled into position, sitting just beneath the global navigation/header. This ensures filters and status remain persistently visible while the user scrolls through the list and detail content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rule-document-list is the dominant element in the main content band beneath the filter bar. It begins after a vertical gap of about 32–40px from the bottom of the filter bar, aligning to the left edge of the content container. On larger screens, the page switches into a split layout: the list occupies approximately the left 7 of 12 columns, equating to about 55–60% of the content width. A 24–32px vertical gutter separates it from the rule-detail-panel on the right. The list is presented as a vertical stack of cards or rows, each spanning the full width of this left column with internal padding of 16–24px and 16–24px of vertical spacing between items. Each item uses bold, all-caps titles and clear division/category tags to create a strong, sports-like visual identity while remaining readable. The list extends vertically and is scrollable along with the page, often running taller than the viewport, while the detail panel stays visually anchored to its right. The top of the first list item is aligned horizontally with the top edge of the rule-detail-panel to strengthen the sense of a single, unified content band. On smaller screens (tablet and mobile), this component expands to use 100% of the width, and the detail panel collapses below it in the stacking order, preserving the single-column, top-to-bottom flow that defines the design DNA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page as the main vertical content stream. On desktop, it remains in a fixed left column, allowing users to scroll through many items while keeping the detail panel visible to the right; on mobile, it simply becomes the top portion of a stacked single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rule-detail-panel resides to the right of the rule-document-list within the same main content band, echoing the hero’s split layout by acting as the right-hand anchor. On desktop, it occupies approximately the right 5 columns of the 12-column grid, or about 40–45% of the content width, starting flush against a 24–32px gutter from the list. The panel’s top edge aligns with the topmost rule list item, so both pieces of content create a strong horizontal line across the page. The panel itself feels like a large, solid card on a dark background, with generous internal padding (24px on all sides) and clear typographic hierarchy: a bold rule title at the top, followed by division/category tags, key metadata, and then the detailed text body. Controls like bookmark/save, share, or copy-link sit in the upper-right of this panel, mirroring the navigation emphasis in the original hero. The panel’s background contrasts with the page body—either darker or slightly tinted—to give it weight and make it a focal point. Its dimensions are tall enough to display a meaningful snippet of rule content without feeling cramped; on taller content, the panel scrolls with the page but is designed to stay within the viewport as much as possible. On wide screens, it behaves with a sticky-like presence: as the user scrolls the list, the panel remains fixed within the viewport until the bottom of the list is reached, so the selected rule is always visible while browsing neighboring items. On tablets and smaller screens, the panel reflows to appear below the list in the single-column stack, spanning the full width, with a 32–40px gap above it to maintain clear separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and large tablets, it uses a sticky or pinned behavior within its column, remaining visible near the top of the viewport while the user scrolls the longer rule list next to it. On smaller screens, it becomes a standard, non-sticky block that scrolls naturally below the rule-document-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-resources-summary is placed beneath the main list/detail band, separated by a strong vertical margin of 48–64px that clearly marks it as a new, self-contained section. It adopts the visual language of the registration strip: a bold, full-width horizontal bar with a solid dark background that can extend full-bleed across the viewport while its content remains centered within the max-width container. Vertically, this strip is slightly shorter than the filter bar—around 64–80px tall—with 16–24px internal padding. Content is organized in a low-density, center-weighted layout. At the center, a headline such as 'MY SAVED RULES' appears in all-caps, bold typography. Directly beneath or slightly to the left, a short line of explanatory text can appear in a smaller size.
