On the far right of the content container, aligned to the rightmost 3–4 columns, a prominent count or action (for example, '7 SAVED' or a 'VIEW ALL' link) is displayed in an accent color, echoing the right-aligned status cue from the registration strip. The left side is intentionally kept cleaner, potentially with a small icon or label, to avoid overcrowding. This band visually and functionally signals a transition from general browsing to personalized content, anchoring the bottom of the rules page’s main experience in a familiar, high-contrast strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section that scrolls naturally; it does not stick but serves as a visual and functional footer for the rules browsing experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The rules-pagination-controller is tightly associated with the rule-document-list and is positioned directly beneath the last visible list item within the left column of the main content band. On desktop, it sits within the same 7-column width as the list, centered horizontally in that column. A vertical gap of 24–32px separates it from the final rule card to avoid visual clutter, with another 32–40px gap below separating it from the my-resources-summary strip. The pagination controls themselves—page numbers, next/previous arrows, and possibly a 'Page X of Y' label—are arranged in a single, compact horizontal row, with the dominant control (such as the current page number or 'Next') visually highlighted in the league’s accent color. The right-hand detail panel continues to the right of this pagination band but is not interrupted by it; the top of the pagination row aligns approximately with the lower third of the detail panel, preserving the impression that they belong to the same overarching content band. On tablet and mobile, the pagination expands to span nearly 100% of the available width and remains placed immediately after the rule list, before the rule-detail-panel in the vertical stacking order, so that users encounter page navigation naturally as they finish scanning the current page of items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control block that scrolls in tandem with the rule-document-list. It does not stick or float; its position at the bottom of the list visually anchors the end of the current result set and naturally invites pagination.

name: My Dashboard
title: My Dashboard
description: A consolidated page where users can see and manage their saved games (My Schedule), volunteer commitments, saved teams, and saved rule/resources, with quick navigation to related pages.

core_features: Load a consolidated snapshot of the user’s saved games, volunteer shifts, teams, and resources using getDashboardSummary.
Allow the user to focus on a specific section (My Schedule, My Volunteer Shifts, My Teams, My Resources) via the URL view parameter and in-page section tabs.
Display a My Schedule list of all saved games with key details and a per-row action to view the game in the main schedule, plus a control to remove games via removeGameFromMySchedule.

Display a My Volunteer Shifts list of all confirmed volunteer commitments with date, time, field, role, and status, including the ability to cancel commitments via cancelVolunteerSignup and a navigation control to find more shifts on volunteer.html.

Display a My Teams list of saved teams, including division, skill level, note, and coach contact info, with ability to remove saved teams via removeSavedTeam and navigate to teams.html to find more teams using the saved team context.
Display a My Resources list of saved rule and policy documents, with each title linking to the corresponding article on rules.html and the ability to remove resources via removeSavedResource.
Provide section-level counts (for saved games, shifts, teams, resources) to indicate how many items exist in each area, using the counts object from getDashboardSummary.
Support section-level refresh using getMyScheduleGames, getMyVolunteerShifts, getMyTeams, and getMyResources so users can update specific sections without reloading the entire page.
user_workflows: View overall dashboard snapshot: (1) User navigates to user_dashboard.html (optionally with a view parameter). (2) On page initialization, the controller component reads the view parameter from the URL. (3) The controller calls getDashboardSummary to retrieve my_schedule, my_volunteer_shifts, my_teams, my_resources, and counts. (4) The dashboard renders the section tabs using counts, and renders each section with the data from getDashboardSummary. (5) If a view parameter is present, the matching section is set as active and the page scrolls or jumps to that section.

Focus on a specific section via navigation: (1) From another page (e.g., schedule.html, volunteer.html, team_detail.html, rules.html, or header links), the user clicks a link to My Dashboard that includes a view URL parameter (such as ?view=my_schedule). (2) user_dashboard.html loads and the controller reads view=my_schedule. (3) Tabs highlight the My Schedule tab as active and the page scrolls to the My Schedule section. (4) All sections are still available, but My Schedule is visually emphasized as the current focus.

Review and manage saved games in My Schedule: (1) In the My Schedule section, the user sees a list of saved games populated from either getDashboardSummary.my_schedule or, if the user triggers a refresh, from getMyScheduleGames.games. (2) The user clicks a game row’s ‘View in Schedule’ link. (3) The click handler constructs a URL to schedule.html with divisionId and teamId derived from the underlying Game and Team entities associated with the saved game. (4) The browser navigates to schedule.html with those parameters. (5) If the user wants to remove a game, they click a ‘Remove’ action on that row. (6) The handler calls removeGameFromMySchedule with the gameId. (7) On success, the My Schedule component refetches the schedule data via getMyScheduleGames and updates the schedule list and the saved_games_count in the local dashboard state.

Review and manage volunteer commitments: (1) In the My Volunteer Shifts section, the user sees a list of shifts from getDashboardSummary.my_volunteer_shifts or, after refresh, from getMyVolunteerShifts.shifts. (2) To cancel a commitment, the user clicks a ‘Cancel Signup’ control for a specific shift. (3) The handler calls cancelVolunteerSignup with the volunteerSignupId from that row. (4) On success, the shift’s status is updated to the returned status_after value, or the shift is removed from the list according to business rules. (5) The section may optionally call getMyVolunteerShifts again to ensure the list is synchronized. (6) To find new volunteer opportunities, the user clicks a ‘Find More Shifts’ button. (7) The handler derives the month parameter from a relevant shift date (for example, from the nearest upcoming shift or another configured selection) and navigates to volunteer.html?month={derivedMonth}.

Review and manage saved teams: (1) In the My Teams section, the user sees a list of saved teams populated from getDashboardSummary.my_teams or from getMyTeams.teams after a section refresh. (2) Each row displays team_name, division_name, skill_level, note, and coach contact details. (3) To remove a saved team, the user clicks a ‘Remove’ action on the row. (4) The handler calls removeSavedTeam with the savedTeamId. (5) On success, the My Teams component calls getMyTeams, updates its list, and recalculates or updates saved_teams_count in local state. (6) If the user wants to search for similar teams, they click a ‘Find More Teams’ action (for example on a selected row). (7) The handler uses the team’s associated division and skill level (derived from the Team entity via team_id) to navigate the browser to teams.html?divisionId={derivedDivisionId}&skillLevel={skill_level}.

Review and manage saved rule/resources: (1) In the My Resources section, the user sees a list of saved resources from getDashboardSummary.my_resources or, after refresh, from getMyResources.resources. (2) Each row displays title, division_code, and category. (3) The user clicks on a resource title to open the full rule or policy on the rules page. (4) The handler navigates to rules.html?articleId={article_id} using the article_id from that row. (5) To remove a saved resource, the user clicks a ‘Remove’ action on that row. (6) The handler calls removeSavedResource with the savedResourceId. (7) On success, the My Resources component calls getMyResources to refresh its list and updates saved_resources_count in local state.

Refresh an individual section without reloading the page: (1) The user clicks a ‘Refresh’ control within a specific section (e.g., My Schedule). (2) The section component calls the corresponding interface (e.g., getMyScheduleGames for My Schedule). (3) The section’s data is replaced with the response, and the controller updates the matching count in its local dashboard state based on the length of the returned array. (4) Other sections remain unchanged.

Change sections using in-page tabs: (1) The user clicks a section tab (e.g., My Volunteer Shifts) in the tab bar. (2) The tab component updates the active section in local state and optionally scrolls to that section. (3) It also updates the URL using the History API or a full navigation so that the view parameter matches the selected tab (e.g., ?view=my_volunteer_shifts). (4) When the URL changes, the controller ensures that on any future reload or deep link, the same section is focused.
interactions: On page load (onInit), read the view parameter from window.location.search, call getDashboardSummary, store the returned my_schedule, my_volunteer_shifts, my_teams, my_resources, and counts in page state, and mark the section corresponding to view as active if provided.
On tab click in the section tabs, update the active view in page state, update the URL’s view parameter to the selected section key, and scroll/bring the corresponding section into view.

On My Schedule ‘Remove’ action, call removeGameFromMySchedule with the bound gameId for that row; if the response.success is true, call getMyScheduleGames to refresh the schedule list and update saved_games_count accordingly.

On My Schedule ‘View in Schedule’ action, derive the divisionId and teamId from the underlying Game and Team related to the saved game (using server-side or controller-level logic based on game_id) and navigate to schedule.html?divisionId={divisionId}&teamId={teamId}.

On My Volunteer Shifts ‘Cancel Signup’ action, call cancelVolunteerSignup with the bound volunteerSignupId; if success, update that row’s status field to status_after or remove the row, and optionally trigger getMyVolunteerShifts to fully refresh the list and total count.

On My Volunteer Shifts ‘Find More Shifts’ action, determine the month parameter from relevant shift data (for example, from the nearest upcoming shift’s date), then navigate to volunteer.html?month={monthValue}.
On My Teams ‘Remove’ action, call removeSavedTeam with the savedTeamId for that row; on success, call getMyTeams to refresh the teams list and update the saved_teams_count in page state.

On My Teams ‘Find More Teams’ action for a particular saved team, derive divisionId from the related Team entity and read skill_level from the row, then navigate to teams.html?divisionId={divisionId}&skillLevel={skill_level}.
On My Resources title click, navigate directly to rules.html?articleId={article_id} using the article_id from that resource row.
On My Resources ‘Remove’ action, call removeSavedResource with the savedResourceId for that row; on success, call getMyResources to refresh the list and saved_resources_count in local state.

On section-level ‘Refresh’ controls (if present), call the corresponding getMyScheduleGames, getMyVolunteerShifts, getMyTeams, or getMyResources interface and replace that section’s data with the new response.

On URL change where the view parameter changes (e.g., via navigation from other pages), re-evaluate the active section in the controller and adjust the current tab and scroll position without requiring a full browser reload when possible.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state control for this page is the URL query parameter view. Supported values are 'my_schedule', 'my_volunteer_shifts', 'my_teams', and 'my_resources'. On initialization, the controller reads view from the URL. If a supported value is present, it sets the matching section as the active/focused section (for example, highlighting the tab and scrolling to that section). If view is missing or invalid, the controller uses a default (such as focusing My Schedule) while still rendering all sections. Whenever the user clicks a section tab, the controller updates the view parameter in the URL so that the current focus is reflected in the address bar; this can be done by pushing a new history state or by full navigation, depending on implementation. The data contents of each section (lists of games, shifts, teams, resources) are stored in in-memory page state and are populated from getDashboardSummary on page load. When a mutating action occurs (removing a game, cancelling a volunteer signup, removing a team, or removing a resource), the page calls the respective interface and, on success, refreshes the affected section via its specific getMy* interface. The updated list replaces the previous section data in state, and the section counts are recalculated from the list lengths or updated alongside the retrieved data. The page does not rely on localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; all persisted information is obtained via the provided interfaces and controlled only through URL parameters and server responses.

components.functionality: Initializes the My Dashboard page, reads URL parameters, orchestrates data loading via getDashboardSummary and section-specific getMy* interfaces, manages active section state, and coordinates updates after user actions.

Renders clickable tabs for My Schedule, My Volunteer Shifts, My Teams, and My Resources, displays item counts for each section, and controls which section is considered active based on the view state.

Displays the My Schedule section including a header, optional refresh control, and the My Schedule list, using data from the dashboard summary or the schedule-specific interface. Handles empty states and delegates row-level actions to its list component.

Renders each saved game as a row with game date, time, division name, field name, and team names. Provides a per-row control to view the game in the main schedule and a control to remove the game from My Schedule.
Displays the My Volunteer Shifts section with a header, optional refresh control, the volunteer shifts list, and a button to find more shifts on the volunteer page.
Renders each volunteer signup with role, field name, date, start and end time, and status. Provides a per-row action to cancel a signup.
Displays the My Teams section with a header, optional refresh control, and the saved teams list, enabling users to review saved teams and access team-related navigation.

Renders each saved team with team name, division name, skill level, optional note, and coach contact fields. Provides a per-row action to remove the saved team and a per-row action to navigate to the teams page using the division and skill level context.
Displays the My Resources section with a header, optional refresh control, and the list of saved rule and policy documents that the user has bookmarked.

Renders each saved resource with title, division code, and category. Each title is clickable and links to the corresponding rule or policy article on rules.html. Also provides a per-row action to remove the resource from My Resources.

Determines for each section whether the corresponding list is empty and exposes flags or messages the UI can use to present an appropriate empty-state message or call-to-action (for example, suggesting navigation to other pages).

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with full-bleed background. Content appears constrained to a centered max-width container with logo aligned to left columns and navigation aligned to right columns.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom stack inside a full-bleed dark background, anchored to a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid. Immediately under the global site hero/header, the dashboard-page-controller establishes a bold dashboard header band. Directly below that, a full-width horizontal strip houses the dashboard-section-tabs, mirroring the registration/status strip from the mockup. Under the tabs strip, the four data sections (My Schedule, My Volunteer Shifts, My Teams, My Resources) are stacked as distinct full-width bands, each containing its own list. Each section follows a simple top row (title + optional actions) and content area (list or empty state), maintaining the single-column vertical band pattern from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom stack inside a full-bleed dark background, anchored to a centered max-width container on a 12-column grid. Immediately under the global site hero/header, the dashboard-page-controller establishes a bold dashboard header band. Directly below that, a full-width horizontal strip houses the dashboard-section-tabs, mirroring the registration/status strip from the mockup. Under the tabs strip, the four data sections (My Schedule, My Volunteer Shifts, My Teams, My Resources) are stacked as distinct full-width bands, each containing its own list. Each section follows a simple top row (title + optional actions) and content area (list or empty state), maintaining the single-column vertical band pattern from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The dashboard header occupies approximately the upper third to 40% of the viewport height beneath the global header, with a strong split: a left-aligned title block (about 3–4 columns), a wide negative space in the center (about 5–6 columns), and a right-aligned compact meta area (about 3–4 columns). Below, the section-tabs strip is shallower (around 10–15% of the viewport height) but spans the full width. The main content sections beneath use the full width for background color but constrain content to the central grid (about 80–90% of the page width, or 10–12 columns). Within each data section, list content generally sits in the center 8–10 columns to keep ample left/right breathing room. Vertical spacing between major sections (tabs strip to first section, between sections) stays in the 40–64px range, preserving clear separation between content bands.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and reflects the mockup’s low-density, high-impact feel. The header area intentionally has very little text and relies on negative space between left and right blocks. The section-tabs strip presents a concise row of tabs with generous padding around each label. Each data section exposes only a small set of list items above the fold (e.g., 3–5 items), with large, tappable rows or cards and comfortable spacing (16–24px padding and gaps) between them. Labels are in all caps or strong uppercase-like styles, with clear hierarchy and no small, cramped text blocks. The overall presentation feels bold and uncluttered despite multiple functional areas.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows a Z-pattern echoing the mockup. The eye first hits the dashboard header on the top-left (‘MY DASHBOARD’ title), then moves horizontally to any right-aligned user snapshot/meta in the header. From there, attention drops diagonally down to the center of the screen where the section-tabs strip runs horizontally, with tab labels centered across the middle of the strip. The selected tab is further emphasized toward the right side of this strip, echoing the right-aligned ‘.Closed’ status in the mockup. From that right emphasis, the eye travels down to the left edge of the first data section (‘MY SCHEDULE’), then scans horizontally across each list row. As users scroll, this pattern repeats band-by-band: section title on the left, content flowing across the width, with clear left-to-right and top-to-bottom cues.

layout.overall_description: The My Dashboard page sits on a dark, full-bleed background, with all content anchored to a centered max-width container that adheres to an implied 12-column grid. Immediately under the global site header, a bold, horizontally split dashboard header band fills roughly the top third of the viewport: on the far left, a strong all-caps ‘MY DASHBOARD’ title block occupies about a quarter of the width; on the far right, a compact summary area for user name and key stats takes another quarter; between them lies a wide, intentional void that amplifies both sides and reinforces the high-contrast, competitive feel. Below this, a lean, full-width horizontal strip becomes the control center for the page: the dashboard-section-tabs sit in the middle of this strip, with evenly spaced tab labels for Schedule, Volunteer, Teams, and Resources. The entire strip feels like a status bar, with the currently selected tab visually amplified on the right half of the tabs row, echoing the heavy right-aligned status text from the mockup. As you scroll down, four distinct horizontal bands stack vertically, one for each area of the dashboard. Each band spans the full width with subtle shifts in background tone to differentiate them, but the actual content is constrained to the central grid. At the top of each band, a bold section heading on the left and a small right-aligned control (like ‘View All’) form a thin header row; beneath that, the associated list stretches across the center 8–10 columns, showing a small number of generously spaced, high-contrast cards or rows. When a list is empty, a centered, well-padded empty state sits in the same area, maintaining the page’s rhythm and density. The result is a strong, layered vertical composition of dark bands that feel like stadium tiers: powerful, uncluttered, and clearly organized around the user’s key actions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard-page-controller defines the entire main content area under the global site hero/header, stretching full-width across the viewport with a dark, full-bleed background. Inside it, a centered max-width container (approximately 1100–1200px wide on large screens) establishes a 12-column grid. At the very top of this container, it creates a dashboard header band taking roughly 35–40% of the viewport height. Within this header band, the content is split horizontally: the leftmost 3–4 columns hold an all-caps page title like ‘MY DASHBOARD’ and possibly a short subtitle stacked vertically, aligned flush-left with generous padding (24–32px) on the top and left edges. The rightmost 3–4 columns house a compact, right-aligned block for user-related meta (such as the user’s name, season label, or quick stats), stacked in 2–3 lines and aligned to the far right edge. Between these left and right blocks, the center 4–6 columns are intentionally left visually light or empty, reinforcing the bold, sparse hero feel observed in the mockup. Below this header band, the controller introduces a strong horizontal separation: about 40–48px of vertical whitespace before the dashboard-section-tabs strip begins. From there downwards, it stacks each data section (my-schedule-section, my-volunteer-shifts-section, my-teams-section, my-resources-section) in order, separated by 40–64px of vertical spacing, ensuring each section reads as its own horizontal layer. On smaller screens, the controller allows the header content to stack: the title block shifts to the top, spanning the full width, and the meta block drops beneath it, centered, while still keeping a generous vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary flow container in normal document flow, defining the central grid and vertical stacking; it does not fix or float itself, but it reserves space for a tall hero-like dashboard header at the top and ensures consistent section spacing as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard-section-tabs component occupies a full-width horizontal strip directly beneath the dashboard header band created by the page-controller. Vertically, this strip is relatively shallow—around 10–15% of the viewport height or roughly 64–96px tall—and it spans edge-to-edge in the browser, with a slightly darker or more saturated black background than the surrounding page to echo the mockup’s registration/status band. Inside the same max-width central container, the tabs are arranged in a horizontal row roughly centered on the page. On large screens, the tab labels (‘MY SCHEDULE’, ‘VOLUNTEER SHIFTS’, ‘MY TEAMS’, ‘RESOURCES’) are aligned along the middle 50–60% of the width (around columns 2–11), each given generous horizontal padding (24–32px) and vertical padding (12–16px) so they feel like bold, nav-like pills or blocks rather than thin underlined text. The entire row sits slightly above the vertical center of the strip, leaving a slim band of empty dark space above and below, reinforcing the status-strip look. The currently selected tab is visually emphasized and slightly pulled toward the right side of the row composition: it may be the rightmost tab, rendered with a bright league accent color and slightly larger or heavier type, recalling the prominent right-aligned ‘.Closed’ text in the mockup. On tablet and mobile, the strip remains full-width, but the tabs can become horizontally scrollable within the same band, still centered, with the active tab anchored more toward the right side of the visible area when possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sticky status/navigation bar: once the user scrolls past the dashboard header, this strip pins to the top of the viewport, maintaining full-width presence and keeping tab navigation visible while the underlying data sections scroll beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-schedule-section is the first main content band under the section-tabs strip, emphasizing its importance. It spans the full browser width with a subtly distinct dark background tone (e.g., slightly lighter or with a faint gradient) to differentiate it from the tabs strip above and the sections below. There is approximately 40–48px of vertical whitespace between the bottom edge of the tabs strip and the top edge of this section. Within the central max-width container, the section content occupies the full 12-column grid but visually feels concentrated in the center 8–10 columns. At the top of the section, a slim header row about 56–72px tall runs horizontally: the ‘MY SCHEDULE’ title appears in all caps on the far left (starting at column 1 or 2), aligned flush-left, with strong type. On the far right of this row (columns 10–12), a small, high-contrast action such as ‘VIEW FULL SCHEDULE’ sits right-aligned in a smaller but still bold style, creating the left-right tension seen in the mockup. This header row has bottom padding of 16–24px before the list content begins. Below it, the my-schedule-list fills most of the remaining section height, constrained primarily to the center 8–10 columns, leaving 1–2 columns of margin on either side to maintain airiness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal document flow immediately under the sticky tabs strip; its top edge scrolls under the tabs as the user moves down the page, maintaining a clear banded hierarchy with consistent spacing from adjacent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-schedule-list sits entirely within the my-schedule-section, directly beneath the section’s header row. Horizontally, it is centered and typically spans about 8–10 columns of the 12-column grid, starting slightly in from the left edge (for example, beginning at column 2 and ending at column 11) to preserve generous side margins. Vertically, it grows with content but initially shows a modest number of rows (e.g., 3–5 upcoming games) before any internal scrolling is needed. Each list item is rendered as a bold, horizontally oriented card or row roughly 72–96px tall, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides, following the spacing system. Within each row, details such as date/time, opponent, and field are spread along a left-to-right axis: the date block anchors on the far left, the middle shows matchup and team info, and the far right may show a status chip or quick action. Rows are separated by 16–24px of vertical space, giving the list a clean, stepped rhythm rather than a dense table-like appearance. The list aligns firmly to the left within its own column area (not centered text), reinforcing the strong left edge seen throughout the design while leaving plenty of negative space to the sides and between items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside the my-schedule-section in standard flow; the list itself does not scroll independently on large screens but contributes to page height. On very small screens, if the list becomes long, it can scroll with the page normally, preserving the same card spacing and centering within the available width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-volunteer-shifts-section appears directly beneath the My Schedule band, separated from it by approximately 48–64px of vertical whitespace to maintain strong sectional separation. It also spans the full width with its own subtly differentiated dark background tone, continuing the stacked-band aesthetic. Inside the central max-width container, it again uses the 12-column grid, with its content visually concentrated in the central 8–10 columns. The top of this section features a header row similar in height to the My Schedule header (about 56–72px tall): on the far left, an all-caps ‘MY VOLUNTEER SHIFTS’ title starts at column 1 or 2, aligned to the left; on the far right (columns 10–12), a smaller control like ‘VIEW ALL SHIFTS’ or ‘MANAGE’ is right-aligned. This creates a consistent left-right tension line across all sections. There is 16–24px of padding beneath this header row before the internal my-volunteer-shifts-list begins, ensuring the content does not feel crowded against the section title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in the vertical stacking of sections, moving naturally with page scroll. It maintains consistent top and bottom spacing relative to adjacent sections and does not fix or float in the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-volunteer-shifts-list is nested within its section, occupying the center 8–10 columns beneath the header row, starting slightly in from the left edge to mirror the My Schedule list’s alignment. It presents each volunteer shift as a bold, horizontally oriented card or row, each with around 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical gaps between items. Information within each row is arranged from left to right: the left side highlights shift date and time, the central area names the role or assignment, and the right side can show a status or quick action (such as ‘Cancel’ or ‘Details’), aligning to the far right of the content area. The rows are tall enough (about 72–88px) to feel touch-friendly and substantial, matching the moderate content density from the mockup. If there are only a few shifts, the list occupies a relatively shallow area in the section, leaving comfortable dark space below; if more, it extends downwards but keeps the same card rhythm and generous spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in standard document flow within its parent section and scrolls with the page. It does not introduce internal scroll bars on most viewports, relying instead on page-level scrolling to reveal additional items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-teams-section follows the volunteer section, separated by roughly 40–64px of vertical whitespace to maintain distinct visual layers. Like the other sections, it is full-width with a slightly varied dark tone to subtly differentiate it from its neighbors. Within the central max-width container, the content adheres to the 12-column grid. The top of the section includes a header row similar in structure and height to the previous sections: the ‘MY TEAMS’ title appears in bold, all-caps on the left, starting around column 1 or 2, while a right-aligned control such as ‘VIEW TEAMS’ or ‘FIND TEAMS’ occupies columns 10–12, aligned flush-right. This header row is padded on top and bottom (around 16–24px) and is followed by a clear gap before the my-teams-list begins, aligning with the overall spacing system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as another layer in the vertical stack, scrolling under the sticky tabs strip as the user moves down, without any fixed or floating behavior of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-teams-list sits beneath the header row of the teams section and is centered in the grid, typically spanning about 8–10 columns. Unlike strictly linear rows, these items can appear as a vertical stack of team cards, each with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between cards, maintaining the same moderate density as other lists. Each team card uses the left side of the card to display the team name and level in bold, while the right side can host a small badge (e.g., ‘FAVORITE’ or ‘CURRENT’) or a quick action button. Cards are arranged in a single column to preserve the strong vertical banding and avoid clutter; each card’s width aligns with the same left edge used for other lists, giving the entire central column a consistent visual line. If there are only one or two teams, they occupy the upper portion of the section, with empty dark space below reinforcing the bold, minimal feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the my-teams-section in normal flow; the list length contributes to the overall height of that section and scrolls with the page, without separate internal scroll behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-resources-section is positioned as the final major band in the main column, separated from the My Teams section above by around 40–64px of vertical spacing. It also spans the full width with a slightly adjusted dark background tone, closing out the dashboard’s stacked-band composition. Inside the central max-width container, it uses the 12-column grid and visually centers its content within the middle 8–10 columns. At the top, the section header row mirrors the pattern established earlier: ‘MY RESOURCES’ appears in strong, all-caps type on the left side (starting at column 1 or 2), and a right-aligned control like ‘VIEW ALL RESOURCES’ or ‘BROWSE’ sits on the far right (columns 10–12). This row has 16–24px of bottom padding before the list begins, and the entire section has generous top and bottom padding (24–32px) to give the ending of the page a comfortable, anchored feel rather than an abrupt stop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in normal document flow at the bottom of the dashboard’s main column; as the user scrolls, it eventually becomes the primary content in view, with the sticky section-tabs strip still visible at the top of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The my-resources-list occupies the central portion of the resources section, typically spanning about 8–10 columns and centered horizontally below the section header. It presents saved rules, documents, or helpful links as vertically stacked resource cards or rows, each with 16–24px internal padding and separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Each item’s left side can show an icon or resource type label with the title immediately to its right, while the far right of the row can hold a small indicator (e.g., ‘PDF’, ‘LINK’) or a chevron to imply navigation. The rows are aligned flush-left within their column area, preserving the strong left edge line established across sections. Even with multiple resources, the generous padding and gaps keep the list from feeling dense, aligning with the mockup’s preference for impact over information overload.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides in standard document flow, scrolling naturally with the page as part of the my-resources-section. The list does not create a separate scroll area and instead relies on the main page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The dashboard-empty-state-handler is not a visible section on its own but defines how empty states appear within each of the four data sections when their lists have no items. Within any given section, it occupies the same horizontal footprint as the list (typically the central 8–10 columns) and is vertically centered within the remaining content area of that section. When active, it replaces the list’s rows with a single, well-padded empty-state panel: a medium-sized icon or emblem can appear at the top of the panel, followed by a bold, all-caps message (e.g., ‘NO GAMES SAVED YET’) and a short, single-line description beneath. The panel spans about 60–70% of the available width and is centered within the list area, with generous top and bottom padding (24–32px) and ample surrounding negative space, keeping the visual density low. The empty-state panel aligns with the same left and right boundaries as list items, ensuring that even when there is no data, the strong central column and left-aligned text rhythm of the dashboard are preserved.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: This logic-driven component appears conditionally inside each data section in place of its list. It does not float or fix; instead, it is centered within the available section space and scrolls with the rest of the page content, maintaining the same banded structure and vertical spacing as if list items were present.
